"jarvis":"I've gone through everything — the architecture diagram (`Jarvis AI OS — v2`) and the full 17-page Hebrew spec. Nothing is missing; together they give a complete picture. Here's my understanding so we're aligned:

**What Jarvis is:** a local "AI OS layer" sitting on top of your machine — a unified client + a hard-coded command engine + an AI orchestrator ("Brain") that plans tasks, dispatches permanent & temporary agents, runs controlled capabilities (files, terminal, browser, media), connects to external services via MCPs/Connectors, manages transparent memory + a RAG knowledge base, and keeps everything safe through Permissions, Approval Center, Secrets Vault, Sandbox, and full audit/observability.

**Core principle from the spec:** *"Jarvis owns the architecture; SDKs are adapters."* So we build the Brain, agent runtime, command engine, permission guard, memory, etc. ourselves — and use SDKs (Claude API, MCP, OAuth, Playwright) only as technical adapters.

**The spec's own recommended stack matches your ask:** Java 21+ / Spring Boot core, React client (optionally wrapped in Tauri/Electron later), with optional Node/Python sidecars for browser automation / OCR / local models.

This is a large system, so the spec wisely lays out an 11-phase roadmap. I strongly suggest we **start at Phase 1 (MVP skeleton)** and build outward — get a working spine first, then layer capabilities. Phase 1 = Jarvis Client + Jarvis Explorer + Command Engine + Input Router + a few slash commands (`/help`, `/jfiles`, `/settings`, `/status`), with the module structure laid out so later phases slot in cleanly.

## What I read
The 17-page Hebrew spec (`Jarvis_AI_OS_Full_Specification_HE.pdf`) and the v2 architecture diagram. Nothing was missing — together they fully define the system. I summarized my understanding of the vision/architecture earlier; the core principle driving our design is *"Jarvis owns the architecture; SDKs are adapters."*

## What I built (Phase 1 skeleton)
Per your choices — Phase 1 MVP, SQLite, React+Vite web, Maven — and the spec's recommended stack:

**Backend** — Java 17 + Spring Boot 4 (`backend/`, package `com.jarvis.*`):
- **Input Router** — classifies input; *slash command always wins* over AI interpretation
- **Command Engine + Registry** — auto-discovers pluggable `CommandHandler` beans, so adding a command later = adding one class
- **Commands** — `/help`, `/jfiles`, `/status`, `/settings`
- **Jarvis Explorer** — virtual root with the standard folders, plus an **allowed-folder / blocked-path guard** (verified: `../../etc` traversal is blocked)
- **System Monitor** — CPU/RAM/disk/JVM snapshot
- **Audit Log** — every action persisted to **SQLite**

**Client** — React 19 + TS (`client/`): a themed Jarvis console with a grouped command palette, file browser, and live status cards.

## Verified
- Backend compiles (`mvnw`), client builds (`tsc + vite`)
- Live browser test: `/status` rendered metric cards, `/help` rendered the command table, all through React → Vite proxy → Spring Boot → SQLite (see the two screenshots above)
- Path-traversal guard, unknown-command handling, AI stub, and audit persistence all confirmed via API

## Notes & how to run
- **Port:** I moved the backend to **8088** — `8080` was already taken by a node process on your machine.
- `README.md` and `.gitignore` written; **git repo initialized but not committed** (I leave the first commit to you).

```bash
cd backend && ./mvnw spring-boot:run     # http://localhost:8088
cd client  && npm install && npm run dev  # http://localhost:5173
```

# Jarvis AI OS — Full Build Roadmap (zero → hero)

Ordered phases for **backend (Java/Spring Boot)** and **client (React/Vite)**. Each phase lists backend work, client work, behind-the-scenes work, and exit criteria. Build phases in order; never skip the cross-cutting concerns — they are what make it production-grade, not a demo.

Legend: ✅ done · 🔜 next · ⬜ planned.

## CROSS-CUTTING CONCERNS (continuous — apply in EVERY phase)
Not a phase; they grow with every feature. A feature's "definition of done" includes the relevant items here.

- **Error handling** — global `@RestControllerAdvice` → consistent error DTO `{ code, message, detail, traceId, timestamp }`; typed `JarvisException` hierarchy (`PermissionDenied`, `PathBlocked`, `ApprovalRequired`, `Connector`…); error→HTTP-status mapping; never leak stack traces; client-side central error handling + retry/backoff.
- **Logging & audit** — structured Logback (JSON in prod), **trace ID per request via MDC** propagated to agents/connectors/audit; disciplined log levels; no secrets/PII in logs; Audit Log distinct from app logs, queryable via `/logs`.
- **Validation** — Bean Validation on all DTOs + command params; client mirrors the rules.
- **Security** — secrets never reach LLM/client (vault adapters only); CORS locked; CSRF/rate-limit/size limits; path & command allow-lists + risk classification; local API auth / loopback binding; dependency scanning.
- **Testing** — backend unit + slice (`@WebMvcTest`/`@DataJpaTest`) + integration (`@SpringBootTest`/Testcontainers); client Vitest + RTL + Playwright E2E; contract tests; all in CI.
- **Config & profiles** — `dev`/`prod`/`test`, env vars, typed `@ConfigurationProperties`.
- **Persistence & migrations** — introduce **Flyway** once entities stabilize (stop relying on `ddl-auto`); keep entities portable for a Postgres move.
- **API & real-time** — REST for req/response, **WebSocket/SSE** for streaming; versioned `/api/v1`; OpenAPI; pagination/envelopes.
- **Observability & health** — Actuator + Micrometer (latency, command counts, agent durations, token/cost); Agent Debugger later.
- **i18n, RTL & accessibility** — **Hebrew + English / RTL from early on**; keyboard nav, ARIA, contrast.
- **Resilience** — timeouts, retries+backoff, circuit breakers, idempotency, graceful degradation.
- **Build, packaging & delivery** — Maven wrapper + npm; Dockerfile; **Tauri/Electron in Phase 11**; CI/CD (lint→test→build→artifact).

## The phases
- **PHASE 0 — Foundations** ✅ — repo, Boot 4 + Java 17, React + Vite + TS, SQLite, wrapper, CORS, README.
- **PHASE 1 — Client shell + Command Engine** ✅ — Input Router, Command Engine + Registry, `/help /jfiles /status /settings`, Jarvis Explorer + guard, System Monitor, Audit Log, REST API. *(Still owed → folded into Phase 2: global error advice, Flyway, trace IDs, real tests.)*
- **PHASE 2 — File access + Permissions** 🔜 — read/write/delete with per-folder perms, `PermissionGuard` + Permission Modes, file content endpoints, `/searchall /clipboard /openapp /logs`; **+ Flyway, error advice, trace IDs, first test suite.** Client: full Explorer UI + internal viewers/editors (text/md/image/PDF/code/CSV/JSON), RTL+i18n scaffolding, error toasts.
- **PHASE 3 — Memory Manager** ⬜ — Memory entity (provenance/confidence/sensitivity/expiry), CRUD+search+export, `/memory`; client Memory screen.
- **PHASE 4 — System Monitor + Real-time** ⬜ — richer metrics, Actuator, **WebSocket/SSE live feed**, `/resources`, local API auth; client live dashboard + notification center.
- **PHASE 5 — Sandbox + Approval Center + Secrets Vault** ⬜ — Approval Center (risk/why/preview/remember, 422 flow), Terminal risk classification, Sandbox Runtime, **Secrets Vault** (encrypted, OAuth, never-expose-to-LLM); `/approve /sandbox /secrets /terminal /stop`.
- **PHASE 6 — Agent System + Jarvis Brain (+ AI adapters)** ⬜ — `jarvis-ai` (Claude/OpenAI adapters, streaming, **tool-calling loop**, context builder), Brain/Orchestrator, own Agent Runtime (permanent + temporary/parallel), Durable Task Engine, Privacy Agent masking; replaces the Phase 1 AI stub. Client: streaming chat + progress, Agents/Tasks screens.
- **PHASE 7 — Connectors / MCPs** ⬜ — `jarvis-mcp` + `jarvis-connectors` (Gmail/Calendar/GitHub/Notion/Drive/Slack/Search), OAuth via vault, Connector Health, resilience; `/plugins` foundation. Client: Connectors screen + OAuth UX.
- **PHASE 8 — Workflow Builder + Scheduler + Durable Tasks** ⬜ — triggers/conditions/steps/approval-gates/retries, Cron, crash recovery; `/workflows /scheduler`; client visual builder + run history.
- **PHASE 9 — Knowledge Base / RAG** ⬜ — indexing/chunking/embeddings, **vector store (likely add Postgres + pgvector)**, semantic search + **citations**, `/kb`; client KB Manager.
- **PHASE 10 — Model Router + Observability / Agent Debugger** ⬜ — router (privacy/cost/latency/quality), Cost/Token Monitor, Evaluator/Critic, full run trace/replay/compare; `/models /costs /debugger /replay`.
- **PHASE 11 — Media, Advanced Automation, Voice, Packaging (hero)** ⬜ — media gen, Browser Automation (Playwright sidecar), OCR/screenshot QA (Python sidecar), **Plugin SDK + manifest + marketplace**, **Voice STT/TTS**, and **package as Tauri/Electron desktop app** with signing/auto-update + release pipeline.

## Open decisions to revisit
Tauri vs Electron (by Phase 11) · Postgres migration timing (Phase 9) · local model on Mac · formal Plugin Manifest · **upgrade JDK 20 → 21 LTS early.**

A few notes on how I structured it:
- **The cross-cutting section is deliberately separate** from the phases — error handling, logging/trace IDs, audit, validation, tests, security, migrations, observability, resilience, i18n/RTL aren't a "phase you finish," they're part of every feature's done-criteria. That's where the behind-the-scenes work you mentioned lives.
- I called out the **debt from Phase 1** (no global error advice, no Flyway, no trace IDs, no test suite yet) and scheduled it into **Phase 2** so it doesn't get forgotten.
- I added things the spec implies but didn't enumerate: **real-time (WebSocket/SSE)**, **Flyway migrations**, **RTL/Hebrew i18n**, **local API auth**, **voice (STT/TTS)**, and **CI/CD + desktop packaging**.

## Backend
- **PermissionGuard + Permission Modes** (`SAFE` / `ASSISTED` / `AUTONOMOUS`, spec §11.1) — every file op is authorized against the active mode, the folder's permission level, and the blocked-paths list, with directory-traversal rejection. Deletes need confirmation in `ASSISTED` mode → returns **422 `APPROVAL_REQUIRED`** (a clean placeholder for the Phase 5 Approval Center).
- **File operations** — read / write / create / delete / search, all guarded, exposed over REST (`/api/files/content`, `/dir`, `DELETE`, `/api/search`).
- **Global error handling** — one `ApiError` envelope `{ code, message, detail, traceId, timestamp }` + a typed `JarvisException` hierarchy; stack traces never leak.
- **Request trace IDs** — every request gets an id in the MDC + `X-Trace-Id` header, flowing into logs and error responses.
- **New commands** — `/searchall`, `/logs`.
- **20 backend tests** pass (guard, file system, command engine, controller slice).

## Client
- **Internal file viewer/editor** — open → edit → save (verified opening `welcome.md`).
- **File actions** — new file / new folder / delete, with hover-delete on rows.
- **`/logs` viewer** — renders the persisted audit trail.
- **Error toasts** showing the message + trace id, and an **LTR/RTL** layout toggle.

I verified the whole flow live (screenshots above): permissioned writes, the 422-on-delete-without-confirm, blocked traversal (403), the editor, and the audit log view.

## Honest notes
- **Flyway deferred to Phase 3.** I'd committed to it for Phase 2, but introducing Flyway over a single audit table on SQLite now would be fragile churn — it's better timed for Phase 3 when the Memory + permission tables land and the schema stabilizes. Backend still uses Hibernate `ddl-auto: update` (tests use `create-drop`). This is recorded in the roadmap memory.
- Also deferred (noted in memory): `/clipboard` and `/openapp` (local-OS actions that fit better with Phase 5's risk/approval model), and rich binary/PDF/CSV viewers (text/markdown editing is done).

README and the roadmap/project memory are updated to reflect Phases 1–2.

## Flyway (the deferred Phase‑2 item) — done
This had a real Boot 4 gotcha worth recording:
- `flyway-database-sqlite` **doesn't exist** — SQLite support is bundled directly in `flyway-core` (the separate "native-connectors" `flyway-database-nc-sqlite` is 12.x-only).
- Boot 4 split autoconfiguration into per-tech modules, so `flyway-core` alone did nothing — I had to add **`org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-flyway`** to wire it in.
- Result: Hibernate `ddl-auto: none`, **Flyway owns the schema** via `db/migration/V1__init.sql` (audit_log + memory). Confirmed in the boot log: *"Successfully applied 1 migration to schema main, now at version v1"*.

## Backend
- **`Memory` entity** (spec Appendix C): id (`mem_xxxxxxxx`), category, title, content, source/provenance, confidence, visibility, sensitivity, created/updated/expires timestamps, enabled.
- **Full CRUD + search + export**, validated DTOs (missing title → 400 with `title: must not be blank`), every mutation audited, `/memory` command.
- **26 backend tests** pass (added `MemoryService` create-defaults/patch/search/expiry).

## Client
- **Memory Manager screen** — cards with category chips, **SENSITIVE** badge, source/confidence/id, enabled state.
- **Add/Edit modal** — confidence slider, sensitivity/visibility selects, expiry datetime, enabled toggle.
- **Delete** and **JSON export download**.
- Verified the full create round-trip live: filled the form → Save → modal closed → `/memory` auto-refreshed → the new memory appeared (screenshots above).

- README + roadmap/project memory updated. Your dev DB now holds a few sample memories from verification — harmless local data.

## How close is our architecture to the diagram?

**Breadth: we've built roughly 20–25% of the diagram's surface area.** But the more important answer is about *fidelity*, and there it's high:

**Architecturally, we are faithful to the diagram — we've built the correct foundational spine, not a random 25%.**

- The diagram's core flow is **Input → Router → (Command Engine | Brain) → Capabilities/Agents → KB/Connectors → Governance/Monitoring → Outputs**. Our package structure mirrors that exact spine: `input/` → `command/` → `explorer/` + `system/`, with `security/`, `audit/`, `memory/`, `error/` as the cross-cutting governance layer. A new block slots into its named place.
- The diagram's stated top principle — *"Jarvis owns the architecture; SDKs are adapters"* — is honored: we built our own command engine, permission guard, audit, and memory rather than leaning on a framework to define them.
- The design invariants the diagram emphasizes are real in code today: **deterministic commands first** (slash always wins), **human-in-the-loop hook** (the 422 approval gate), **transparent memory** (full CRUD + provenance), **observable** (trace IDs + audit on every action), **controlled file access** (allowed/blocked + modes).

**Where we diverge from the diagram is by design and timing, not by architecture:** the entire **right/intelligence half** — Brain/Orchestrator, Model Router, the 30+ Agents — and the **integration layers** — Connectors/MCPs, KB/RAG, Sandbox, Secrets Vault, Workflows — are not built yet. Those are the phases ahead (5–11). None of them require re-architecting what exists; they attach to the spine we already have.

So: **think of the diagram as the finished house, and we've poured the foundation + framed the load-bearing walls + wired the safety systems.** The rooms (agents, connectors, RAG, workflows) aren't built, but the structure they'll hang on is in place and matches the blueprint.

One small caveat worth flagging: the diagram shows ~28 slash commands and a rich set of internal viewers; we have 8 commands and text-only viewing. Those are breadth items that fill in cheaply as their backing capabilities land — not architectural gaps.

## Backend
- **Risk Classification** — `RiskClassifier` rates commands LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH/CRITICAL by pattern. Verified: `echo` → LOW, `npm install` → MEDIUM, `git push`/`curl|sh` → HIGH, `rm -rf`/`sudo shutdown` → CRITICAL.
- **Approval Center** — a sensitive action is submitted with risk + preview + "why" and parked as PENDING (the deferred action held in memory); **approve runs it**, **deny drops it**, and "remember this decision" auto-decides future actions of that type. Full audit trail persisted.
- **Sandbox Runtime** — runs commands in an isolated, throwaway working directory with a timeout and captured output. Verified: exit codes, output capture, and timeout all work.
- **`/terminal`** ties it together: classify → require approval → run in sandbox.
- **Secrets Vault** — AES-GCM encrypted credentials. Verified: ciphertext in the DB (no plaintext), API returns only masked `••••cret`, and every access is audited. Plaintext is reachable **only** by internal adapters, never over HTTP or to the model.
- **41 backend tests** pass.

## Client
- **Approval Center** — pending cards with colored risk badges, preview, Approve/Deny + Remember. Verified live: approving `echo` produced a **Sandbox · 7 ms · exit 0 · SUCCESS** block with the output, and the list auto-refreshed to leave the CRITICAL `rm -rf` pending.
- **Secrets Vault** — masked list + add modal (password value, "encrypted on save") + revoke.

## What is already REAL (not mocked)
These run real logic against real data — no fakery:
- **File system** — real `java.nio` reads/writes/creates/deletes/search.
- **Permission Guard + modes**, **blocked paths**, **traversal guard** — real enforcement.

- **Audit log, Task history** — real persistence (SQLite + Flyway migrations, all real).
- **System Monitor** — **real** JVM/OS metrics (`OperatingSystemMXBean`) streamed over **real SSE**.
- **Approval Center** — real: persisted requests, the deferred action actually runs on approve, remembered decisions work.
- **Sandbox** — **real**: it genuinely executes shell commands via `ProcessBuilder` in an isolated working dir with a real timeout and captured output. (The *isolation* is lightweight — a temp cwd, not a container — but the execution is 100% real.)
- **Secrets Vault** — **real** AES-GCM encryption, real ciphertext at rest, real masking.
- **Agent runtime / tool-calling loop** — real orchestration.
- **The 5 tools** (`list_files`, `read_file`, `search_files`, `system_status`, `memory_search`) — **real**: they call the real services and return your real data.
- **Connector framework** — registry, health-from-vault, invoke, audit are all real.

## The TWO genuine mocks (and why)
1. **`MockLanguageModel`** — the AI "brain" is a heuristic stand-in, *not* real intelligence. The real path is `AnthropicLanguageModel`, which **requires your Anthropic API key** and which I currently left **text-only** (no tool-use).
2. **Connector data** — the *framework* is real, but the 4 connectors return canned sample data instead of calling the real Gmail/GitHub/Slack/Calendar APIs (which need **your OAuth tokens/credentials**).

I built those two as mocks **on purpose, and disclosed it each phase**, for one reason: so the whole system runs and is testable **offline with no secrets**. Making them real is not a code trick — it fundamentally requires *your* credentials, and some (Google OAuth) need real setup I can't fabricate or verify without you.

A couple of things are **real but deliberately simple** (not mocks): risk classification and agent selection are rule-based, not ML — that's the spec's "deterministic commands first" principle, not a placeholder.

So: I'm fully on board to make the mocked parts real. To build the *right* real thing rather than a pile of code I can't verify, I need a few decisions:"

"jarvis":"**Phase 8 — Workflows + Scheduler + Durable Tasks — complete and verified end-to-end.** ⚙️ All real logic.

## Backend
- **Durable workflow engine** — a workflow is a list of steps (`COMMAND`, `BRAIN`, `CONNECTOR`, `NOTIFY`, `APPROVAL`), each calling a **real** capability. The engine runs them sequentially and **persists run state to SQLite after every step**, with **per-step retries**.
- **Approval gates that durably pause/resume** — an `APPROVAL` step pauses the run and opens an Approval Center request; approving it **resumes the run from where it stopped** (ties Phase 8 to Phase 5). Verified: gated workflow → PAUSED → approve → DONE.
- **Real cron scheduler** — `SCHEDULE` workflows fire on a Spring `CronTrigger` via a `TaskScheduler`. Verified live: a `*/3 * * * * *` workflow fired runs every 3 seconds (visible in the runs timeline).
- **Triggers**: MANUAL / SCHEDULE / WEBHOOK; `/workflows` + `/scheduler` commands. **55 backend tests** pass (run, retry→fail, approval pause→resume).
- One DI knot resolved: `WorkflowEngine`'s `CommandEngine` dependency is `@Lazy` (it would otherwise form a cycle through the command registry).

## Client
Verified live: the Workflow Builder lists workflows with Run/Delete, a **+ New workflow** modal with a per-type step builder, and a **runs timeline** showing the cron firing with per-step status dots (screenshots above).

## Honest deferrals (in memory)
- **Crash auto-resume of mid-flight RUNNING runs** (approval-pause resume works; recovering a step that was executing when the JVM died isn't wired yet).
- Conditions/branching, parallel steps, idempotency keys, and FILE_CHANGED / EMAIL_RECEIVED / voice triggers.

"jarvis":"Great input — and the "agent, not robot" point is the most important thing here, so let me anchor on it: **the design rule for every feature below is that it becomes a *tool/capability the Brain can choose*, not a hard-coded keyword route.** That's exactly the difference between your old Jarvis and this one. I'm **not implementing yet** — here's the analysis + phase split you asked for. Pick what you want and I'll build it.

## Your direct questions (3, 4, 7)

**3. Does Jarvis handle memory of *you*?** Yes — Phase 3 Memory Manager stores facts about you (preferences, projects, rules) with provenance/sensitivity/expiry, fully user-editable, and the Brain injects your top memories into every request as context. **But** today memories are only created *manually* (UI/API). Jarvis doesn't yet *decide on its own* to remember things from a conversation — that's #4.

**4. Does it "learn"/evolve chat-to-chat?** Partially. Memory persists across sessions and is used as context. **Missing:** (a) **automatic memory formation** — the agent noticing "this is worth remembering" and proposing/saving a memory, and (b) **conversation continuity** (#8) — right now each message is handled statelessly. These two are the heart of "agent, not robot," and both attach to phases we've already built (Memory + Brain), so they can come first.

**7. Did I leverage macOS / Apple Silicon, or hard-code?** Honestly: **neither yet, really.** The only OS-level execution is the sandbox running `sh -c`. I have **not** built macOS-native capabilities (`open -a` to launch apps, `open -R` to reveal in Finder, `screencapture`, `pbcopy/pbpaste`, `mdfind`/Spotlight, `sips` for images, `say` for TTS, `osascript`). The sandbox *can* run them, but there's no agent tool around them. This is a big opportunity — and the right way (per your philosophy) is to expose each as a **tool the Brain picks**, not a hard-coded command.

## Items that map to ALREADY-BUILT phases → these come first

| # | Feature | Where it fits | Effort | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 9 | Move `vaultKey` default out of Java into properties only | Phase 5 (done) | tiny | Pure cleanup |
| 6 | Configurable Jarvis Explorer root | Phase 2 (done) | tiny | **Already exists** (`jarvis.filesystem.jarvis-root` / `JARVIS_ROOT`) — just surface it in `/settings` UI |
| 1 | "Open file location" button in viewers | Phase 2 | small | macOS `open -R <path>` capability + button |
| 7 | **macOS Local Actions as tools**: open app, reveal in Finder, clipboard, screenshot, Spotlight search | Phase 2/5 (Local Actions capability) | medium | The big "make it Mac-native + agentic" win |
| 8 | **Conversation continuity** (follow-ups use prior turns) | Phase 6 Brain | medium | Persist session turns, feed to the Brain |
| 4/3 | **Auto-memory formation** (Brain proposes/saves memories) + a `memory_write` tool | Phase 3+6 | medium | Makes it "learn" |
| 5 | **Notification Center + bell** (persistent, not just toasts) | Phase 4 (done) | medium | Workflows/approvals push real notifications |
| 2 | More slash commands + aliases (`/files`,`/explorer`,`/history`,`/config`,`/today`,`/projects`…) + a `write_file` agent tool | Phase 1 Command Engine | small–med | But keep it agentic — aliases, not 100 hard-coded routes |

## Items that belong to UPCOMING phases

| Feature(s) | Phase |
|---|---|
| Semantic search, "find a doc by meaning", **ask your documents** (RAG Q&A), "catch me up / what was I working on" | **Phase 9 — Knowledge Base / RAG** |
| Model routing, cost, observability/agent-debugger | **Phase 10** |
| Screenshot→file, **create PDF / Word**, image convert/resize (`sips`), **web search / research / when-questions**, **browser automation**, **voice + wake-word ("Jarvis") + TTS**, "build me an app", media generation, the floating-sphere/nav-rail UX | **Phase 11** |
| Mail **draft/send**, calendar brief, GitHub search as Brain-invoked flows, **natural-language reminders** ("remind me at 3pm") | Mostly *work already* once a real model is wired (they're connector/workflow calls the Brain chooses) — small connector additions (Gmail draft) + a NL→reminder path on Phase 8 |

## What we genuinely DON'T have yet (the gap list)
RAG / semantic doc Q&A · macOS app-launcher / reveal / clipboard / screenshot / Spotlight · web search & browser automation · voice/STT/TTS & wake word · PDF/Word/image creation · auto-memory formation · in-session conversation continuity · Notification Center · mail draft/send · NL reminders · "build an app" scaffolding · client UX (Cmd-K, Esc, nav rail, sphere, extra screens like Today/Projects/History) · "open file location" button.

## The one cross-cutting reframe
Almost everything above (mail, calendar, GitHub, screenshots, app launching, reminders, multi-step) **already half-works architecturally** — the agent runtime + tool loop can chain them. What made the old Jarvis robotic was hard-coded routing; here the gap is mostly **(a) wiring each capability as a Brain tool, and (b) a real model that decides to use them.** So a high-leverage move is: build the macOS + memory + web tools, and they all become things the agent *figures out on its own*.

Let me save this as a backlog so it's not lost:"

## What I built
- **Pluggable `EmbeddingModel`** (mirrors the `LanguageModel` pattern) with a default **offline `HashingEmbeddingModel`** — hashing-trick + sublinear TF + L2-normalize → genuine cosine ranking, **no API key and no model download**. A neural embedder drops in behind the interface later.
- **Index → chunk → embed → store**: `KnowledgeBaseService` indexes a file, a folder, or ad-hoc text; chunks (~1000 chars, 150 overlap); embeds each chunk; persists vectors in SQLite (migration **V6**). Re-indexing a source replaces it.
- **Semantic search with citations** — cosine over chunks returns the source doc + snippet + score.
- **Ask-your-documents is agentic, not a hard-coded pipeline**: a **`kb_search` tool** wired into the Research/Knowledge/General agents, so the Brain *decides* to retrieve and answers with sources.
- `/kb` command + `KbController` + a client **Knowledge Base screen** (index, scored search, document list).

## Verified live
- Indexed 2 docs → **semantic search** for "enterprise cost" ranked the **Q4 plan** doc top.
- Chat *"according to my documents, what is the enterprise pricing?"* → **Research Agent → `kb_search` → answer cited `[Q4 plan]`**.
- UI search showed **Q4 plan at 97%**. **58 backend tests pass.**

## What I deliberately left for later (and why)
- **Neural embeddings** — today's embedder is real *lexical* IR (great offline, no key), not semantic-neural. The real neural embedder slots in behind `EmbeddingModel` when you wire a key — and that's the natural moment to introduce **Postgres + pgvector** for scale (right now cosine runs in-process over SQLite, perfect for local size).
- **Durable ingestion + auto re-index on file change**, codebase-aware chunking, and privacy controls on indexed content — these are refinements that pair with the durable-task and permissions work.
- **"Catch me up / what was I working on"** activity summarization — small, fits here but better once it can summarize across KB + tasks.

## What I built
- **Model Router** — a `ModelCatalog` (local mock + Claude tiers with cost/quality/latency metadata; the active adapter is flagged "available") and a **policy-based `ModelRouter`** (`BALANCED` / `PRIVATE` / `QUALITY` / `CHEAP`, set via `jarvis.ai.privacy`) that scores and picks among *available* models — so the choice is real, not cosmetic. Plus `CostCalculator`.
- **Observability** — every Brain run is now persisted as an **`AgentRunRecord`** (migration V7): agent, model, request/answer, status, prompt+completion tokens, **cost**, **duration**, and the full **step trace**. The Orchestrator routes a model per request (adds a "Routed to model…" trace step), times it, computes cost, and records it.
- **Agent Debugger** (`/debugger`) — run list, expandable trace, and **`/replay <id>`** which re-issues the original request.
- **Cost / Token Monitor** (`/costs`) — totals + breakdown by model and by agent.
- `/models` shows the catalog. Client screens for all three. **64 backend tests pass.**

## Verified live
- Chat trace shows **"Routed to model mock-local (BALANCED)"**; runs persisted with tokens/cost/duration; `/costs` aggregated (320 tokens, by-agent); `/replay` re-ran a recorded request; the **Debugger UI** expands the full trace per run (screenshot above).

- **Evaluator/Critic agent + run *compare*** view.
- **Real multi-model routing** only kicks in once more than one adapter is wired (offline there's just `mock-local`; the routing *logic* is real and unit-tested, but has one available model until you add an API key or a local model).

"jarvis":"Great review — these are exactly the right questions to ask before the codebase gets big. Let me answer the conceptual ones honestly, then I've got two genuine decisions for you before I run the refactor batch.

## Your two big questions

**"Spine" — is that all, or 100% of the diagram?** Not 100%. Be clear-eyed about it:
- **By architecture: ~100%.** Every major *block* in the v2 diagram exists and runs for real (Input→Router→Command Engine+Brain→agents→tools/capabilities→connectors→KB→governance→model router→observability→monitor→voice→plugins).
- **By item count: ~55–65%.** The *lists inside* several blocks are partial:
  - **Commands:** ~complete (29, meets/exceeds the diagram's list).
  - **Governance/safety, monitor, brain:** near-complete.
  - **Capabilities:** ~22 of ~31.
  - **Agents: 8 of ~28.** ← thin (your #3/#8).
  - **Connectors: 4 of ~30.** ← thinnest.

So #8 is **correct**: many things you predefined exist as a *working category* but not the full enumerated set. The honest gaps are agents and connectors.

## Per-item responses

1. **Split tool classes** — agree, will do (one file per tool, like `ConnectorTool`). `Tools.java`/`MacTools.java`/`WebTools.java` → individual `*Tool` files.
2. **AgentSelector** — small correction: `slug = "general"` is a **string literal**, so it's interned once in the constant pool — it does *not* allocate on the heap per call, so there's no memory cost. But the *readability* point stands; I'll refactor to a clean rule-list/switch with early returns. (Good instinct, just not a memory issue.)
3. **All the agents** — yes, I'll add the full permanent-agent roster from the spec/diagram (~28).
4. **When to delete `MockLanguageModel`** — the moment you commit to *always* having a real model: i.e., you add an `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` (or a local model) and want Claude as a hard default that fails loudly if missing. Until then it's the only thing keeping the app runnable/testable offline. Give me a key and I'll delete it (or hide it behind a `dev` profile).
5. **Single entry point** — you're essentially **right**: the *conversational* entry already is single — you just talk, and `POST /api/chat` (the Brain) decides what to trigger. The many other endpoints are (a) **deterministic slash commands** ("slash always wins" — a deliberate fast/safe bypass) and (b) **data feeds for the UI panels** (file browser, memory screen, debugger…). Those aren't the "talk to Jarvis" path. So: one brain entry + a supporting REST layer for screens. Not wrong at all.
7. **`JarvisFilesystemProperties`** — (1) I'll rename → `JarvisFileSystemProperties` (capital S). (3) `jarvisRoot` is already bound from `jarvis.filesystem.jarvis-root` in the yml — but it still has an in-code default; I'll remove that so it's property-sourced only (like we did for `vaultKey`). (2) getters/setters depends on the Lombok decision below.
9/10. **Hardcoded values → properties** — agree, I'll sweep them all into config (`ConversationService` history size, `MAX_TEXT_BYTES`, `MAX_SEARCH_RESULTS`, KB chunk size/overlap, sandbox timeout, vault hint length, etc.) under a typed `@ConfigurationProperties` with self-describing names + a short comment only where the name isn't obvious.
11. **Packages** — fair question. What I did is **package-by-feature** (`command`, `memory`, `kb`, `workflow`…), which is a legitimate, scalable style. As it grows, grouping cross-cutting ones helps — e.g. `capability.{files,terminal,sandbox,local}`, `ai.{model,embedding,tools}`, `governance.{security,approval,secrets,audit}`, `platform.{config,error,logging}`. Optional — your call below.

- **Packages: keep flat** (package-by-feature).

"jarvis":"I read all four. Here's the honest assessment — **the headline is that our Jarvis already implemented the thing those docs describe as their unbuilt future.**

## The big realization
`ZERO_TO_HERO.md` §17–18 is a long confession that their old Jarvis is *"pattern-match-first, AI-last"* — a regex intent-parser front door with the LLM as a fallback. Their entire "agentic redesign vision" (F10.1: `agent-loop.ts`, model-as-orchestrator, tool-use, clarifying questions) is **exactly what we built from day one** with the "agent not robot" principle + the Anthropic tool-use loop in `AgentRuntime`. So we're not behind these docs — on the core architecture we're a generation ahead of them.

## What we already match
Tool/capability registry with typed specs + risk levels, Approval Center + EXECUTE-style gating + permission modes, Secrets Vault, Memory + auto-extraction, KB/RAG (covers their `ask-document` + `semantic-search`), model catalog/router/**cost dashboard**, observability/replay/**debugger**, **workflow engine + cron**, Notification Center, SSE live monitor, connectors (GitHub/Slack/Gmail/Calendar), screenshot/clipboard/spotlight/say/image-convert.

## Genuinely new ideas worth stealing
I grep-confirmed we **don't** have these:

**Safety & control (real, local, fits cleanly):**
- **Undo / Rollback** — each mutating tool records a reverse recipe; `/undo` walks the audit log back. Completes our safety story (we have the audit log + approvals already; this is the missing third leg).
- **Global kill switch** — one flag checked at every step in the agent/tool loop + workflow engine to halt *everything* in flight. We have per-task `/stop`, not a panic button.
- **Declarative restrictions policy** — externalize allow/deny rules (paths, shell-command patterns, hosts) into editable config that `PermissionGuard` reads. Today risk is hard-coded in `RiskClassifier`.

**New agent skills (real, no cloud key needed):**
- **Project Intelligence** — scan `~/Projects`/`~/Developer`, detect type via `pom.xml`/`package.json`/`Cargo.toml`, "open my X project" → launches the right IDE. Very on-brand macOS tool.
- **Document generation** — `create_pdf`, `create_docx`, `create_diagram` (Mermaid), `ocr_image` (macOS Vision). All doable with Java libs / native, real logic.
- **"Jarvis Today" digest** — aggregates calendar + pending approvals + recent audit + tasks into a morning briefing (reuses our connectors + scheduler).
- **Configurable JARVIS personality** — a persona block (British, dry wit, concise) prepended to the system prompt. Cheap, huge feel-difference.
- **Chat-history search** — FTS over `conversation_turn` (already persisted) as `/find`.

## What shipped

**1. JARVIS personality** (`JarvisPersonaProperties` → `AgentRuntime`)
A British/dry-wit persona block is now prepended to every agent's system prompt. Tunable via `jarvis.persona` in `application.yml` (`enabled` toggle + overridable `prompt`); on by default.

**2. Declarative restrictions policy** (`jarvis.policy` → `RestrictionPolicy`)
The hard floor beneath the Approval Center — editable in config, no recompile:
- `denied-commands` (regex) — a match **never runs**, even if approved. Enforced at the `SandboxService.run()` choke point via a new `PolicyDeniedException` (403). Ships with sane defaults (`rm -rf /`, fork bomb, `mkfs`, raw-disk overwrite).
- `denied-hosts` — `FetchUrlTool` refuses matching hosts.
- `risk-rules` — user-defined `(pattern → level)` rules merged into `RiskClassifier`.
- New `/policy` command shows the active policy + permission mode.

**3. Project Intelligence** (`com.jarvis.project` + 2 tools)
`ProjectScanner` walks `~/Projects`/`~/Developer`/`~/Code`, detects type by marker file (pom.xml→maven, package.json→node, Cargo.toml→rust, `*.xcodeproj`→xcode, …), and `open_project` launches it in the right IDE (configurable `ide-by-type`). Tools `list_projects` + `open_project` wired to the General and DevOps agents.

**4. Document generation** (`DocumentService` + 4 tools) — all real:
- `create_pdf` → PDFBox (with word-wrap + pagination)
- `create_docx` → Apache POI
- `create_diagram` → saves Mermaid source, renders via `mmdc` when installed (degrades cleanly)
- `ocr_image` → macOS Vision via an embedded Swift script (degrades cleanly off-Mac)

Outputs land in the Explorer's `Generated/` folder.

**5. "Jarvis Today" digest** (`DigestService` + `daily_digest` tool + `/today` command)
Aggregates today's calendar (via the Calendar connector), pending approvals, active/recent tasks, and recent activity into a briefing — reusing existing services as a thin read-model.

## Where we landed vs. the old docs
The most important takeaway remains: their docs spend pages describing an *aspirational* agentic redesign (model-as-orchestrator, tool-use loop) that **our Jarvis already had**. These five additions were the genuinely new capability gaps — now closed. The bigger-ticket items I flagged but you didn't pick (rollback/undo, kill switch, proactive triggers, browser automation, the `/build` coding pipeline, multi-channel push, LAN/auth, HUD redesign) are still on the table whenever you want them.

Note: the new tools surface to the agent and backend now, but I haven't touched the React client — if you'd like UI surfaces (e.g. a "Today" panel or a Projects screen), that's a natural next step."

## 1) "To hero" audit of the v2 diagram

Honest status against the real codebase — **✅ real**, **🟡 partial**, **⛔ not built**. Roughly **~70% of the diagram is real**; I'll be candid about the rest.

**✅ Real & solid**
- **Input layer** — voice (Web Speech STT), chat, slash; **TTS + spoken summary**
- **Command Engine** — registry, ~32 handlers, parser, voice aliases, permission guard, command audit
- **Jarvis Brain / Orchestrator** — intent→agent-select→model-route→context-build→tool-loop→task+observability, now **streaming**
- **Model Router/Manager** — catalog, privacy/cost/latency/quality prefs, fallback, **local+cloud (mock/claude/ollama)**, embeddings
- **Memory + Safety + Governance** — Memory CRUD, Audit Log, Permissions, Allowed/Blocked paths, **Safe/Assisted/Autonomous modes**, Task History, Risk Classification, **Restrictions Policy**, session/long-term memory + provenance
- **Approval Center** — pending/approve/deny, risk, remember-decision, preview
- **Secrets Vault** — AES-GCM encrypted, scopes, revoke
- **Agents** — full **~34 permanent roster** + temporary-agent factory
- **Agent Debugger / Observability** — run trace, prompt/tool trace, replay, timing, tokens, cost, failures
- **Knowledge Base / RAG** — indexing, chunking, semantic search, citations, retrieval (SQLite vectors)
- **Capabilities core** — file system + Explorer, terminal, app launcher, web search, summarization, notifications, system monitoring, local actions, screenshot, **OCR**, clipboard, spotlight, **doc-gen (PDF/DOCX/Mermaid)**, digest
- **Workflow engine** — durable runs, steps, approval gate, retries, cron scheduler
- **System Monitor** — CPU/RAM/Disk/health/active-agents/running-tasks/connector-health
- **Outputs** — chat, TTS, notifications, file results, status, spoken summary

**🟡 Partial (works, but not "to hero")**
- **Planner / Task Decomposition / Decision Engine** — it's **single-agent + tool-loop**, not a true multi-agent **DAG planner** or parallel decomposition
- **Temporary agents** — factory exists; no parallel-work/results-merge orchestration
- **Sandbox** — lightweight (temp dir + timeout + output capture + policy deny), **not container-isolated**, no "promote after approval"
- **Workflow triggers** — cron + manual real; **webhook / email-received / file-changed** triggers not wired
- **Plugin/Extension SDK/Marketplace** — introspection surface only; no dynamic loading or MCP-server hosting
- **Secrets** — static tokens; no **OAuth / refresh-token** lifecycle
- **Voice** — browser Web Speech, not Whisper/ElevenLabs
- **File viewers/editors** — backend serves content, but the new HUD doesn't have the viewer windows yet (the old console did)

**⛔ Not built (the real gaps)**
- **MCPs / Connectors breadth** — only **4 real** (GitHub/Slack/Gmail/Calendar). The other ~20 + actual **MCP-protocol servers** (Postgres/Docker/K8s/Playwright/Notion/Drive/Stripe/Home Assistant/RSS/Maps/Spotify) aren't built. *This is the biggest gap.*
- **Browser automation (Playwright)** — web fetch is static HTML only
- **Media generation** (image/video) and a **multimodal/vision model**
- **Screenshot comparison**, **inbound webhook server**, **GPU/local-model telemetry**, real **Backup/Restore**

So: the **spine, brain, safety, memory, workflows, observability, RAG, and core capabilities are real**. The honest "to hero" gaps are **connector/MCP breadth, multi-agent planning, browser automation, media gen, and OAuth**.

## 2) Where the routing "pipeline" lives

Your instinct is right, and it **does exist** — it's just not the only door right now. The filter layer is:

```
POST /api/command  →  CommandController
   → CommandEngine.execute(input, sessionId)
       → InputRouter.route(raw)          ← THE pipeline / decision point
           ├─ "/known"   → SLASH_COMMAND → CommandRegistry → handler
           ├─ "/unknown" → error
           └─ anything else → AI_REQUEST → Orchestrator (Brain: agent + model + tools)
```

`com.jarvis.input.InputRouter` is exactly the "decide where this goes" layer from the diagram's **Input Router** box, and `CommandEngine` dispatches accordingly. Slash → deterministic handler; free text → the Brain. One filter, server-side.

**Why you see "so many endpoints":** two different kinds are mixed together:
- **The cognitive entry** — `/api/command` (router → command or brain) and `/api/chat` + `/api/chat/stream` (straight to the Brain).
- **REST resource endpoints** — `/api/files`, `/api/memory`, `/api/workflows`, `/api/secrets`, `/api/runs`, `/api/status`, … These exist **only so the UI windows can fetch/manage their own data** (list memories, read a file, show runs). They're plumbing, not the thinking path. That separation is normal and healthy.

**The one wrinkle:** the new HUD currently makes the slash-vs-text decision **in the client** (the dock sends slash text to `/api/command` and free text to `/api/chat/stream`), so today it's *two* doors, not the single server-side router you pictured.

If you want it to match your mental model exactly — **one entry that filters everything** — I can add a single streaming endpoint, e.g. `POST /api/input`, that runs `InputRouter` server-side and either executes the command or streams the Brain, so the client has exactly **one cognitive door** and the resource endpoints stay purely for data. Want me to consolidate it that way?"

"shay":"f you want it to match your mental model exactly — one entry that filters everything — I can add a single streaming endpoint, e.g. POST /api/input, that runs InputRouter server-side and either executes the command or streams the Brain, so the client has exactly one cognitive door and the resource endpoints stay purely for data. Want me to consolidate it that way?
if this is the right way to go (and i think it is, but am not sure how it would affect the performance) - then yeah i wanne u to do it. but first lets analyze the performance 

those r ur next steps - 
Grounded in the code now. Here are both answers.
1) "To hero" audit of the v2 diagram
Honest status against the real codebase — ✅ real, 🟡 partial, ⛔ not built. Roughly ~70% of the diagram is real; I'll be candid about the rest.
✅ Real & solid

* Input layer — voice (Web Speech STT), chat, slash; TTS + spoken summary
* Command Engine — registry, ~32 handlers, parser, voice aliases, permission guard, command audit
* Jarvis Brain / Orchestrator — intent→agent-select→model-route→context-build→tool-loop→task+observability, now streaming
* Model Router/Manager — catalog, privacy/cost/latency/quality prefs, fallback, local+cloud (mock/claude/ollama), embeddings
* Memory + Safety + Governance — Memory CRUD, Audit Log, Permissions, Allowed/Blocked paths, Safe/Assisted/Autonomous modes, Task History, Risk Classification, Restrictions Policy, session/long-term memory + provenance
* Approval Center — pending/approve/deny, risk, remember-decision, preview
* Secrets Vault — AES-GCM encrypted, scopes, revoke
* Agents — full ~34 permanent roster + temporary-agent factory
* Agent Debugger / Observability — run trace, prompt/tool trace, replay, timing, tokens, cost, failures
* Knowledge Base / RAG — indexing, chunking, semantic search, citations, retrieval (SQLite vectors)
* Capabilities core — file system + Explorer, terminal, app launcher, web search, summarization, notifications, system monitoring, local actions, screenshot, OCR, clipboard, spotlight, doc-gen (PDF/DOCX/Mermaid), digest
* Workflow engine — durable runs, steps, approval gate, retries, cron scheduler
* System Monitor — CPU/RAM/Disk/health/active-agents/running-tasks/connector-health
* Outputs — chat, TTS, notifications, file results, status, spoken summary
🟡 Partial (works, but not "to hero")

* Planner / Task Decomposition / Decision Engine — it's single-agent + tool-loop, not a true multi-agent DAG planner or parallel decomposition
* Temporary agents — factory exists; no parallel-work/results-merge orchestration
* Sandbox — lightweight (temp dir + timeout + output capture + policy deny), not container-isolated, no "promote after approval"
* Workflow triggers — cron + manual real; webhook / email-received / file-changed triggers not wired
* Plugin/Extension SDK/Marketplace — introspection surface only; no dynamic loading or MCP-server hosting
* Secrets — static tokens; no OAuth / refresh-token lifecycle
* Voice — browser Web Speech, not Whisper/ElevenLabs
* File viewers/editors — backend serves content, but the new HUD doesn't have the viewer windows yet (the old console did)
⛔ Not built (the real gaps)

* MCPs / Connectors breadth — only 4 real (GitHub/Slack/Gmail/Calendar). The other ~20 + actual MCP-protocol servers (Postgres/Docker/K8s/Playwright/Notion/Drive/Stripe/Home Assistant/RSS/Maps/Spotify) aren't built. This is the biggest gap.
* Browser automation (Playwright) — web fetch is static HTML only
* Media generation (image/video) and a multimodal/vision model
* Screenshot comparison, inbound webhook server, GPU/local-model telemetry, real Backup/Restore
So: the spine, brain, safety, memory, workflows, observability, RAG, and core capabilities are real. The honest "to hero" gaps are connector/MCP breadth, multi-agent planning, browser automation, media gen, and OAuth.
2) Where the routing "pipeline" lives
Your instinct is right, and it does exist — it's just not the only door right now. The filter layer is:

```
POST /api/command  →  CommandController
   → CommandEngine.execute(input, sessionId)
       → InputRouter.route(raw)          ← THE pipeline / decision point
           ├─ "/known"   → SLASH_COMMAND → CommandRegistry → handler
           ├─ "/unknown" → error
           └─ anything else → AI_REQUEST → Orchestrator (Brain: agent + model + tools)

```

`com.jarvis.input.InputRouter` is exactly the "decide where this goes" layer from the diagram's Input Router box, and `CommandEngine` dispatches accordingly. Slash → deterministic handler; free text → the Brain. One filter, server-side.
Why you see "so many endpoints": two different kinds are mixed together:

* The cognitive entry — `/api/command` (router → command or brain) and `/api/chat` + `/api/chat/stream` (straight to the Brain).
* REST resource endpoints — `/api/files`, `/api/memory`, `/api/workflows`, `/api/secrets`, `/api/runs`, `/api/status`, … These exist only so the UI windows can fetch/manage their own data (list memories, read a file, show runs). They're plumbing, not the thinking path. That separation is normal and healthy.
The one wrinkle: the new HUD currently makes the slash-vs-text decision in the client (the dock sends slash text to `/api/command` and free text to `/api/chat/stream`), so today it's two doors, not the single server-side router you pictured.
If you want it to match your mental model exactly — one entry that filters everything — I can add a single streaming endpoint, e.g. `POST /api/input`, that runs `InputRouter` server-side and either executes the command or streams the Brain, so the client has exactly one cognitive door and the resource endpoints stay purely for data. Want me to consolidate it that way?"

"jarvis":"You're right to check perf before committing — and I should be explicit: I'm **not** going to spin up a multi-agent workflow here (that "workflow" keyword is just from my quoted audit text, not a request to orchestrate). This is a focused analysis. Let me get **real numbers** off the running backend rather than hand-wave."

"jarvis":"**Verdict from real measurements (loopback, mock model):**

| Path | Latency |
|---|---|
| Slash command (`POST /api/command` `/status`) | ~7 ms (38 ms cold) |
| Slash `/jfiles` (real work) | ~8 ms |
| AI blocking (`POST /api/chat`) | ~14 ms |
| **AI streaming (SSE `/api/chat/stream`)** | **~13 ms** |

The headline: **SSE costs essentially nothing vs blocking** (~13 ms vs 14 ms) — on loopback the connection/framing overhead is sub-millisecond. The latency is dominated by the actual work, and with a *real* LLM (seconds), streaming **improves** perceived latency, never hurts it. So routing through a streaming door is performance-safe.

The real (non-latency) considerations, and how I'll handle them:
1. **A thread is held for each open stream** → I'll use a **bounded** executor (not the unbounded cached pool), so a burst can't spawn unlimited threads. Single-user local, so this is comfortable.
2. **EventSource is GET-only → input rides in the URL** (~8 KB limit, shows in access logs). Fine for commands and normal sentences; I'll **keep the existing `POST /api/command` + `/api/chat`** as-is for long/programmatic payloads. The new door is additive, not a removal.
3. **Window-open shortcuts stay instant** — typing `/today` will still open the Today window client-side (pure UI nav, no round-trip); only actual *requests* go through the door.

So: **one cognitive door, server-side routing, zero meaningful perf cost.** Building it now.

**Backend — one streaming entry that runs `InputRouter` and either runs the command or streams the brain:**"

## Performance analysis → verdict
Measured on the running backend (loopback, mock model):

| Path | Latency |
|---|---|
| Slash command | ~7–8 ms |
| AI blocking (`/api/chat`) | ~14 ms |
| AI streaming (SSE) | ~13 ms |

**SSE costs ~nothing vs blocking** (sub-ms framing on loopback), and with a real LLM streaming *improves* perceived latency. So the single-streaming-door design is performance-safe. The only real (non-latency) risks were thread count and GET-URL length — handled by a **bounded executor** and by **keeping `POST /api/command` + `/api/chat`** for long/programmatic payloads.

## What I built — the one cognitive door
- **`CommandEngine.execute(raw, session, onStep)`** — the routing brain now has a streaming-aware entry: `InputRouter` decides command vs AI; the AI path streams each step.
- **`GET /api/input/stream`** (`InputController`, bounded thread pool) — the single door. Streams `step`* → `result` → `done`. A slash command returns one `result` (no steps); free text streams the agent's thinking then the result.
- **Client**: `streamInput()` + the dock now sends **everything through this one door**. Window-opener slashes (`/today`, `/memory`, …) stay instant client-side nav (no round-trip); everything else — free text *and* commands like `/policy`, `/status`, `/workflows` — flows through the door and renders in the response window (chat → streamed steps + answer; command → clean `DataView`, as the `/policy` screenshot showed).

So your mental model now holds: **one server-side filter (`InputRouter`) behind one door**, with the REST resource endpoints (`/api/files`, `/api/memory`, …) staying exactly as they were — pure data plumbing for the windows, not the thinking path.

Verified end-to-end: free text streamed `intent→agent→model→answer` then the answer; `/policy` rendered as a structured policy view; backend tests green."

```
POST /api/command  →  CommandController
   → CommandEngine.execute(input, sessionId)
       → InputRouter.route(raw)          ← THE pipeline / decision point
           ├─ "/known"   → SLASH_COMMAND → CommandRegistry → handler
           ├─ "/unknown" → error
           └─ anything else → AI_REQUEST → Orchestrator (Brain: agent + model + tools)


```

`com.jarvis.input.InputRouter` is exactly the "decide where this goes" layer from the diagram's Input Router box, and `CommandEngine` dispatches accordingly. Slash → deterministic handler; free text → the Brain. One filter, server-side. Why you see "so many endpoints": two different kinds are mixed together:

* The cognitive entry — `/api/command` (router → command or brain) and `/api/chat` + `/api/chat/stream` (straight to the Brain).

Want me to consolidate it that way?"

"jarvis":"Important clarification — and no, I'm **not** spinning up a multi-agent workflow here (that "workflow" keyword is just from your pasted audit text; this is a conceptual question). Let me answer it straight.

**Short answer: yes for *thinking*, no for *data* — and that's the correct design.**

What changed: there's now **one cognitive door** — `/api/input/stream` — that every *user request* flows through. Type a command or ask a question, it hits that one endpoint, the backend `InputRouter` decides (command vs Brain), and it streams back. That's the "single entry that filters everything" you pictured, and **it's done and verified** (free text streams `intent→agent→model→answer`; `/policy` came back as a structured view; 77 tests green).

What did **not** change (on purpose): the ~90 other endpoints are still there — and they *should* be. They're a **different category**:"

"jarvis":"It's **56 endpoints across 20 controllers** (not 90 — that was a loose figure). Here's the right way to think about it:

**Two categories, deliberately separate:**

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 YOU →  │  ONE cognitive door:  /api/input/stream      │  ← "where should this think?"
        │  → InputRouter → command OR streamed Brain   │     (commands + questions)
        └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 UI  →  │  ~55 resource endpoints (data plumbing)      │  ← "give me / change this data"
 windows│  /api/files /api/memory /api/runs /api/status│     (list memories, read a file,
        │  /api/secrets /api/workflows /api/settings …  │      fetch run traces, telemetry)
        └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

So: **one door for *thinking*, many doors for *data*** — and that's correct, not a leftover. The resource endpoints are addressable nouns (a memory, a file, a run, a secret). Collapsing them into one endpoint would just rebuild a worse router *inside* a giant handler — more complexity, not less. Nobody designs REST that way; even ChatGPT/Claude have one chat endpoint plus dozens of resource endpoints behind the scenes.

**What the consolidation actually achieved** (and it's done): every *request you make* — slash command or natural-language question, typed or spoken — now enters through the single `/api/input/stream` door, and the backend decides where it goes. Before, the client guessed slash-vs-text and hit two different endpoints; now the **decision is one place, server-side** (`InputRouter`). That was the real fix, and it's verified live (free text streams the step trace; `/policy` returns a structured result; 77 tests green).

The window-fetch endpoints stay as they are because the Memory window asking "list my memories" isn't a *request to Jarvis* — it's the UI reading its own data. Different job, rightly a different door.

So to answer directly: **yes, you now have a single cognitive entry** that filters every request — and the ~55 data endpoints remain as the (correct) plumbing beneath the windows. Nothing more to consolidate there; doing so would be a step backward."

## "When" — the honest answer
There's no calendar here; "when" = **we knock these out one focused session at a time, in priority order.** I can't truthfully promise dates, but I can sequence them by leverage and size so each session ships a real chunk. Here's the plan:

| # | Phase | Size | Why this order |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Connectors breadth + real MCP client** | L | Biggest gap. A real MCP-protocol client lets Jarvis use *any* MCP server (Postgres, Docker, **Playwright browser**, filesystem…) **and** adds Notion/Drive/Sheets/Jira/RSS/Maps/Stripe/Home-Assistant. Unlocks several other gaps at once. |
| 2 | **OAuth / refresh-token lifecycle** | M | Makes Gmail/Calendar/Drive actually work without hand-pasted tokens. Pairs with #1. |
| 3 | **Multi-agent Planner / DAG + parallel agents** | M–L | The real "build me X" pipeline (plan→code→test→merge). Now genuinely useful since Ollama/Claude give real reasoning. Self-contained, no external deps. |
| 4 | **Browser automation + media gen + vision** | M | Playwright (or via the MCP from #1), image generation, multimodal/vision model. |
| 5 | **Sandbox hardening + workflow triggers + webhook server** | M | Docker isolation + promote-after-approval; webhook/email/file-changed triggers; inbound webhook receiver. |
| 6 | **Voice upgrade + HUD file viewers + telemetry + backup** | M | Whisper/ElevenLabs, file viewer/editor windows, GPU/network stats, real Backup/Restore. |
| 7 | **Plugin SDK + marketplace** | L | Dynamic extension loading — the riskiest, so last. |

That's the path from ~70% → "to hero."

My recommendation for **what to do first**: either **#1 (MCP client — highest leverage, most "OS-defining")** or **#3 (multi-agent planner — fully local, immediately impressive with Ollama)**. The rest stack naturally after.

2 things about this - 

1. fix the error itself
2. when for somereason we do get an error - it should render nicely for a user that dont know shit about html/coding/json and needs to see simple message that rendered beautiful and explains the error in simple words 

then we will move to the next phases"

"jarvis":"Two real problems here, both worth fixing properly. Let me look at the web search first:"

"jarvis":"Found it. The service hits DuckDuckGo's API **with no `User-Agent` header** → DDG's nginx returns `403` + that giant HTML page, and `RestClient` puts the whole HTML body into the exception message, which then surfaced to you. Two fixes:

**Fix 1 — make web search work + never leak HTML:**"

"jarvis":"Web search now sends a browser UA and returns a clean one-liner on any failure — no more HTML leak. Now **Fix 2 — render errors beautifully for a non-technical user.** Adding client helpers + an error card. First the helpers and error styles:"

"jarvis":"Now thread `status` through and use the error card in both result windows:"

"jarvis":"Now the `ResultWindow` (⌘K/nav) and `runCmd` get the same treatment:"

"jarvis":"Now the error-card CSS:"

"jarvis":"Typecheck client, restart backend, then verify the web-search fix + error card:"

"jarvis":"TSC clean. Restart the backend and verify the web-search fix returns a clean message:"

"jarvis":"Web search is fixed — the User-Agent resolved the 403; it returned a real Bitcoin summary. Now verify the **friendly error card** renders. Reloading and triggering an error via the dock:"

"jarvis":"The friendly error card renders perfectly — ⚠ **"Something went wrong"** + plain message *"Unknown command: /nope. Type /help for the list."* No HTML, no JSON, no codes. Let me confirm tests still pass (I touched `WebSearchService`):"

"jarvis":"Both fixes are done and verified — **77/77 tests green**.

**1. Web search fixed (the actual bug):** the root cause was a **missing `User-Agent`** — DuckDuckGo's edge returns a 403 HTML page to header-less clients, and `RestClient` stuffed that whole page into the exception message. Now the service sends a browser UA + `Accept: application/json`, and on *any* failure returns a short human line (logged technical detail stays in the logs). Verified live: *"search bitcoin"* now returns a real summary, no HTML.

**2. Errors render beautifully for non-technical users:** a friendly **error card** (⚠ "Something went wrong" + plain-English message) now shows for any failed command/agent run — confirmed with `/nope` → *"Unknown command: /nope. Type /help for the list."* A `friendlyError()` translator turns technical errors into human language ("A service declined the request — it may be rate-limiting us…", "That timed out…", etc.), and `isHtmlish()/stripHtml()` are a hard safety net so **HTML/JSON can never leak to the screen again** — even an unexpected page becomes "The service returned an unexpected response. Please try again."

Quick memory note, then onward:"

"jarvis":"Both fixes shipped, verified, and noted in memory. ✅ Web search works (real results, no HTML leak) · ✅ errors render as a clean friendly card with HTML/JSON stripped as a hard safety net · 77/77 tests green.

That clears the two issues you flagged. **Next I'll move on to Phase 2 — MCP client + connector breadth** (the biggest remaining gap), per the roadmap I'm driving (①✅ Planner → **②MCP/connectors** → ③OAuth → ④browser/media/vision → ⑤sandbox/triggers/webhook → ⑥voice/viewers/telemetry/backup → ⑦plugin SDK). I'll build the real MCP-protocol client + a few first-party connectors, keep it degrading gracefully when no MCP server is installed, and report back at the next verified checkpoint."

"shay":"continue.."

"jarvis":"On it — **Phase 2: real MCP client + connectors.** Starting with the MCP-protocol client core (JSON-RPC over stdio), the manager, the agent-facing tools, and graceful degradation when no server is configured. Let me set up the task and build."

"jarvis":"Now the MCP client — JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio, structured so the framing is unit-testable:"

"jarvis":"The manager — connects to configured servers at boot with bounded timeouts, degrades gracefully:"

"jarvis":"**79 tests pass** (real JSON-RPC framing verified). Now let me prove the **live stdio transport** end-to-end against a real separate process — I'll spin up a tiny MCP stub server, point Jarvis at it, and confirm discovery works:"

"jarvis":"Now point Jarvis at the stub, restart, and verify live discovery:"

"jarvis":"**Live MCP transport confirmed** — Jarvis launched the stub as a separate process, did the real `initialize` + `tools/list` handshake over stdio, and `/mcp` shows it: `Connected MCP servers: stub` → `echo — Echo back text`. Combined with the unit test proving `tools/call` framing, the full protocol works. Now revert the config to the clean default (empty) so the repo degrades by default:"

"jarvis":"Mark Phase 2a done and record it in memory while the backend restarts:"

"jarvis":"Phase 2a (the hard part) is **done and verified both ways**: live against a real stdio process, and graceful degrade with none configured.

## Phase 2a complete — real MCP client
- **`mcp/McpClient`** — JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio (`initialize → notifications/initialized → tools/list → tools/call`), newline-framed, stream-decoupled so it's unit-testable.
- **`mcp/McpManager`** — launches each configured server as a child process at boot, does the handshake with **bounded timeouts** (a hung server can't stall startup), caches tools, runs calls one-at-a-time per stdio stream, and **degrades gracefully** (none configured / fails to start → logs and carries on).
- **Agent tools** `mcp_list` + `mcp_call`, a dedicated **MCP / Integrations Agent**, and a **`/mcp`** command — all behind the single cognitive door.
- **Config** `jarvis.mcp.servers` (empty by default, with a commented filesystem-server example).

**Verified:** a real `python3` MCP stub connected over stdio → *"MCP server 'stub' connected — 1 tool(s)"*, `/mcp` listed its `echo` tool; reverted to clean config → *"No MCP servers connected…"*. So **any** MCP server (filesystem, Postgres, Playwright/browser, …) now mounts via three lines of config. 79 tests green.

**Next (Phase 2b):** I'll add a few first-party **connectors** (Notion, Google Drive, Sheets, RSS, Maps) on the existing `Connector` framework — same real-HTTP pattern as GitHub/Slack/Gmail/Calendar — then move to **Phase 3 (OAuth)** which makes the Google ones work end-to-end. Continuing down the roadmap."

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Spring Boot 4.0.6, Java 17, Maven wrapper (`./mvnw`), port 8088 (binds 127.0.0.1), package `com.jarvis.*`, SQLite + Flyway V1–V7, Lombok (`@Getter/@Setter`, `@RequiredArgsConstructor`, `lombok.config` copies `@Lazy`/`@Qualifier`/`@Value`).
   - React 19 + Vite + TypeScript client, port 5173, proxies `/api` → 8088. **SSE does NOT survive the Vite dev proxy** — EventSource hits backend `http://localhost:8088` directly (CORS allows :5173). Monitor uses `/api/status` polling.
   - LanguageModel adapter pattern: Mock (offline default), Anthropic (tool-use), **Ollama** (new), **ProviderSwitchingLanguageModel** (picks per-call from `jarvis.ai.provider`, graceful fallback to mock).
   - Streaming agent runs (SSE): AgentRuntime/Orchestrator emit `Step` events via `Consumer<Step>`; `/api/chat/stream` and `/api/input/stream`.
   - Single cognitive door: `InputRouter` → `CommandEngine.execute(raw, session, onStep)` → command OR streamed Brain. REST resource endpoints (~55) are separate UI data plumbing.
   - Multi-agent Planner/DAG (heuristic decomposition + parallel sub-agents + merge).
   - MCP protocol client (JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio, newline-delimited).
   - OAuth 2.0 authorization-code flow + refresh, tokens encrypted in Secrets Vault.
   - Workflow triggers: MANUAL/SCHEDULE(cron)/WEBHOOK/FILE_CHANGED (JDK WatchService).
   - Docker-isolated sandbox (opt-in, `--network none`) with lightweight fallback.

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **Web search 403 HTML leak** (user-reported): DuckDuckGo nginx 403s header-less requests; RestClient put the HTML body in the exception message. Fixed: added User-Agent + clean error message; client `isHtmlish`/`stripHtml`/`ErrorCard` as safety net.
   - **Bell opened new window each click** (user-reported): `result` windows are intentionally non-singleton. Fixed by a dedicated singleton notifications window, then per user's follow-up, replaced with a popover anchored under the bell + "SHOW ALL" → window.
   - **Maps geocode returned empty**: double-encoding — pre-encoded query + RestClient re-encoded `%`. Fixed by using `.uri(b -> b.path("/search").queryParam("q", query)...)` lambda.
   - **CommandEngineTest failed** after adding 3-arg `execute`: test stubbed 2-arg `orchestrator.handle`. Fixed: `when(orchestrator.handle(anyString(), anyString(), any()))`.
   - **OAuthServiceTest redirect_uri assertion** wrong hand-encoding. Fixed by asserting on `URLDecoder.decode(url)`.
   - **DocumentServiceTest** `init()` package-private cross-package. Fixed by mocking `FileSystemService.getRoot()`.
   - **HMR stuck after rapid edits** (UI showed stale/0%): fixed via full `location.reload()`.
   - **`.bell` selector not found** during preview eval: the new HUD bell uses `.iconbtn` (not `.bell`) — test selector was wrong, app was fine.
   - **PolicyHandler import** — `JarvisSecurityProperties` is in `com.jarvis.config` not `com.jarvis.security`.

"jarvis":"**Phase 7 done — 86 tests green, verified with a genuine external JAR via both REST and the HUD.** All real, no mocks:

**Plugin SDK (dynamic JAR loading)**
- **SPI** `com.jarvis.plugin.spi.JarvisPlugin` — the stable contract an external plugin implements (id/name/version/description + `List<Tool> tools()`). Plugins compile against the core `Tool`/`ToolSpec` types.
- **`PluginManager`** scans `<jarvisRoot>/Plugins/*.jar` at startup, loads each in its **own `URLClassLoader`**, discovers implementations via Java **`ServiceLoader`** (`META-INF/services`), registers their tools into the live `ToolRegistry`, and grants them to the General agent — so a dropped-in JAR adds capabilities **without a rebuild**.
- Made `ToolRegistry` thread-safe (`register`/`unregister`/`has`) and added `AgentRegistry.grant/revokeToolsFromGeneral`.

**Local marketplace**
- `catalog.json` lists installable plugins. Install (by id or path) does a **real JAR copy + hot-load**; uninstall unregisters the tools, revokes the agent grant, closes the classloader, and deletes the JAR.
- REST `GET /api/plugins[/installed|/catalog]`, `POST /api/plugins/install`, `DELETE /api/plugins/{id}`; `/plugins [catalog|install|uninstall]` command; `/plugins` surface now reports installed plugins.

**Client** — a **Plugins window** with Installed + Marketplace sections (Install/Uninstall, tool pills) + dock hint.

**Proof it's real:** I built an actual external plugin JAR *outside* Jarvis (`hello-plugin`, contributing a `greet` tool) and:
- `PluginManagerTest` loads it from test resources, **executes** `greet`, asserts the agent grant, and uninstalls — automated, green.
- Live end-to-end: empty boot (0 plugins) → catalog shows it → **install by id** (jar copied, hot-loaded) → `greet` appears in the live tool surface → **uninstall via UI** (tool removed, jar deleted, plugin returns to marketplace).

All test artifacts cleaned; the test-resources JAR is kept so the unit test stays runnable. Memory updated.

"jarvis":"**Phase 8 done — 90 tests green, verified live with a real local model.** The planner is no longer just heuristic:

**LLM-driven DAG planner** (`brain/Planner`)
- **Two tiers, both offline-safe:** for compound/complex requests it asks the model for a JSON plan (`steps[]` with `id` / `task` / `dependsOn`); for short single-intent requests it takes a fast path with **no model call**; and any unparseable/garbage model output falls back to the heuristic connective split — so the system never depends on a particular model.
- Robust parsing: `extractJsonObject` does a balanced-brace scan (tolerates prose and code fences around the JSON), and `sanitizeDependencies` keeps only backward edges on known ids — so the plan is **guaranteed acyclic** and execution always terminates.

**Dependency-aware execution** (`Orchestrator.executePlan`)
- Each step is a `CompletableFuture` gated on its dependencies (`allOf`). Independent steps still run **in parallel**; dependent steps **wait** and receive their upstream steps' outputs as context. The plan step now reports *"pipeline — dependencies honored"* vs *"running in parallel"*.

**Proof it's real:** with Ollama (`llama3.2:3b`), *"research green tea, then summarize, then list follow-ups"* produced a genuine 3-step **pipeline** that executed in order — research → summarize (built on the research) → follow-up questions (built on the summary) — with **zero mock fallbacks**. `PlannerTest` covers the fast path, DAG construction, cycle/unknown-dep stripping, and the garbage→heuristic fallback.

Along the way I caught a real config gotcha: the default `jarvis.ai.ollama-model` is `llama3.1` (not installed on this machine), which silently fell back to mock — noted in memory, and the provider/model is reset to the clean `mock` default. Backend is running clean.

**Roadmap now:** the substantial no-keys-needed work is built. What truly remains is **key-gated / native**: Whisper/ElevenLabs voice, GPU telemetry, the EMAIL_RECEIVED trigger (needs IMAP creds), and hardening extras (per-plugin sandboxing, remote plugin registry). I can take on any of those if you provide the relevant keys/decisions — otherwise say **go** and I'll pick the next best buildable improvement (e.g. surfacing the DAG pipeline visually in the HUD history window, or a planner-driven Workflow generator)."
