"jarvis":"Got it — pausing the build. Let me investigate each item against the actual code so my answers are accurate, not guesses."

"jarvis":"Thanks — I dug into the actual code for each item. Here's the honest status, root cause, and fix for every point.

## Bugs

**1. Chat window "doesn't work" / doesn't record everything**
Partly real. There's no dedicated *chat/transcript* window — when you type in the dock, each message spawns a throwaway "Jarvis" response window (see #2). Server-side, conversation **is** recorded: `Orchestrator` writes both `USER` and `ASSISTANT` turns per session (`ConversationService`), and continuity works *within a page load*. But: (a) the `sessionId` is regenerated on every browser reload, so history resets; (b) **slash commands aren't recorded** (only Brain/chat turns are); (c) there's **no GET endpoint** to fetch the transcript, so nothing shows it. **Fix:** a real singleton Conversation window that accumulates the running transcript + a `GET /api/conversation/{session}` and a stable, persisted sessionId.

**2. Same window opens twice / each task = new step window** — confirmed, and it's by design today. In `openWindow`, `result` and `response` windows are deliberately *non-singleton*, and `askInput` always creates `response-${id}`. So a non-window slash (e.g. `/backup`) typed twice = two windows, and every new task = a new step window. **Fix:** make the conversation/response window a **singleton** that re-renders in place and appends each task's step-tree, instead of spawning a new window. (Window-opening slashes like `/memory` are already singletons — that part's fine.)

**5. Settings has no Save button** — not data loss: `SettingsWindow` saves *on change* (provider switch calls the API immediately; voice prefs write to localStorage). So closing the window keeps your changes. But you're right that it's unclear — there's no confirmation and no explicit Save. **Fix:** add a Save/confirmation affordance (and I'll fold in good settings from your old-version screenshot once you post it).

**6. Mic doesn't work** — it uses the browser **Web Speech API** (`startPTT`). It needs a Chromium browser + microphone permission; in the embedded preview it's typically unavailable. On real Chrome at `localhost` it should prompt for mic access. If it still fails there, that's the known limitation of browser dictation — the real upgrade is **Whisper** (currently deferred as key/native). I'll confirm whether it's a permission issue vs a code bug once you tell me which browser you tried.

**7. The button near the bell** — it's a **manual "⟳ Refresh"** status button (top-right, left of the 🔔). It does work (re-fetches `/api/status`), but it looks like nothing happens because status already auto-polls every 3s — so it's redundant. Recommendation: **remove it.** Easy.

## Config changes you asked for

**3. Move Jarvis's "C: drive" to `~/Documents/jarvis_drive`** — one-line change: `jarvis.filesystem.jarvis-root: ~/Documents/jarvis_drive`. Jarvis recreates its folders there on boot.

**4. Let Jarvis explore the machine's Documents + open/edit files in floating windows** — needs: (a) an **allowed-folder** entry for `~/Documents` with `READ_WRITE`; (b) the Explorer window to browse allowed folders, not just the virtual root; (c) **type-aware viewers** (today everything renders as text — images/PDF would show garbage), so I'd add image/PDF/code viewers; (d) an **"Open file location"** button per opened file (the backend `/api/files/reveal` already exists — just not wired into the file viewer header). This is the biggest item — real read/write to your Documents. ⚠️ Security note: this gives Jarvis (and any active model/plugin) write access to your real Documents; I'll keep the blocked-paths guard in front of it.

## Questions answered

**8. Screenshot — yes, both exist.** Slash `/screenshot` (alias "take a screenshot") and an agent **`screenshot` tool** (`MacActions.screenshot` → saves to the Screenshots folder). Via voice/free-text, a *real* model will call the tool; with the **mock** model it may not (see the agentic note below). The `/screenshot` slash always works directly.

**9. Phase 4 (media gen / vision / screenshot-compare)** — correct, still not built; deferred as key-gated/external. Browser automation is reachable via a Playwright MCP server. No change.

**10. The Sandbox comment is STALE — good catch.** The comment says container isolation is "a later hardening step (Phase 11)", but **Phase 5 already added it**: opt-in Docker `--network none` isolation (`jarvis.limits.sandbox-docker=true`, mem/cpu capped), with the lightweight temp-dir as the default fallback. The *capability* is done; the *comment* was never updated. I'll fix the comment. (Still deferred: the two-stage "promote-after-approval" flow.)

## Agentic vs. "robotic"?

Honest answer: **the architecture is agentic; whether it *behaves* agentically depends on the active model.**
- **Genuinely agentic** (the model chooses): the Brain tool-loop, the Phase 8 DAG planner (I verified it live on Ollama — it decomposed "research → summarize → questions" into a real dependency pipeline), connectors-as-tools, plugin tools, memory writes. On **Ollama/Claude** these are real reasoning.
- **Robotic by design** (and correct): slash commands are deterministic; that's the "explicit command" half of "agent not robot."
- **Robotic seams that are still v1:** (a) the **default `mock` model** — out of the box it returns canned stubs, so it *looks* robotic until you switch to Ollama/Claude; (b) the **`AgentSelector`** picks which agent by keywords, not by model reasoning.

So: switch the provider to Ollama (and fix the `ollama-model` default — it points at `llama3.1`, which isn't installed; should be `llama3.2:3b`) and Jarvis is meaningfully agentic.

### What you can do with Jarvis right now
Talk to it (free text → Brain picks an agent + tools, or a multi-step **DAG pipeline**); run ~30 slash commands; **files** (read/write/search the virtual Explorer), **memory** (durable facts, auto-formed), **system monitor** (CPU/mem/disk/**network throughput**), **macOS actions** (open apps, clipboard, **screenshot**, Spotlight, reveal in Finder, `say`), **web** search + fetch, **knowledge base** (RAG), **connectors** (GitHub/Slack/Gmail/Calendar/Notion/Drive/RSS/Maps), **OAuth**, **workflows** (manual/cron/webhook/file-change triggers), **approval center** + **secrets vault** + **sandboxed terminal**, **document generation** (PDF/Word/diagram/OCR), **/today** digest, **MCP** servers, **plugins** (drop-in JARs + marketplace), **backup/restore**, and observability (run traces, costs, replay).

### Next features I'd build (your "which one" menu)
- Surface the **DAG pipeline visually** in the History window (show the dependency graph + per-step outputs).
- A **planner-driven Workflow generator** (turn a described goal into a saved Workflow).
- **Model-based agent routing** (replace the keyword `AgentSelector` with model reasoning — kills the last robotic seam).
- The **Conversation/chat window** + transcript persistence (fixes #1/#2 properly).
- **Documents access + rich file viewers** (#4).

Nothing is coded yet — you're in review mode. Where do you want me to start?"

"jarvis":"Here's the path in the UI (the change I just shipped):

1. **Open the Explorer** — in the bottom dock, click the **`Files`** button (or type `/files`).
2. At the top of the file list you'll now see a **`📂 Documents`** entry (that's your real macOS `~/Documents`, mounted read-write). Click it.
3. You're now browsing the actual OS Documents folder — you'll see your real folders (`DEV`, `jarvis_drive`, `Jarvis_exports`, …). Click folders to go deeper; the breadcrumb shows `Explorer / Users / shaymarko / Documents / …`, and **↑ Up** walks back out.
4. **Click any file** to open it in the right-hand pane:
   - text/code → editable, with a **Save** button
   - images → inline preview
   - PDFs → inline preview
5. Each opened file has an **Open location** button (top-right of the viewer) that reveals it in Finder.

Two notes:
- If you don't see the `📂 Documents` entry yet, **hard-reload the page** (Cmd-Shift-R) so the client picks up the new build.
- Going **↑ Up** *above* `Documents` (into `/Users/shaymarko`) will show a friendly "out of scope" message — that's the permission guard doing its job. Click **Explorer** in the breadcrumb to jump back to Jarvis's own root.

Your "C: drive" (`jarvis_drive`) also lives *inside* Documents, so you can reach it either from the Jarvis root or via `📂 Documents → jarvis_drive`.

## How to reach OS Documents files
`Files` (dock) → **`📂 Documents`** at the top → drill into folders → click a file. You've already got that working — your breadcrumb shows you're inside `Programming lessons - John Bryce`. ✅

## Why videos didn't render (and the fix)
My viewer only knew images/PDF/text — video files fell into the text branch, which tried to read the binary as UTF‑8 and broke. I added proper media support:

- **Backend:** the `/api/files/raw` endpoint is now **HTTP Range‑aware** — it returns `206 Partial Content` with `Content-Range` and streams in ~1 MB chunks instead of loading the whole file into memory. Verified on your real files: a **7.6 GB `.mov`** responded `206` with a correct range, and an **`.mp4` played in‑app** (loaded to `readyState 4`, 180s, 1280px).
- **Client:** a `fileKind()` router now renders each type correctly:
  - image → `<img>`
  - **video → `<video controls>`** (seekable)
  - audio → `<audio controls>`
  - PDF → `<iframe>`
  - text/code → editor + Save
  - anything else → a clean "No inline preview — use Open location" fallback (no more garbage/errors).

One **codec caveat**: `.mp4`/`.webm` play in Chrome; `.mov` (QuickTime) often won't decode in Chrome even though it streams fine — that's a browser limitation, not Jarvis. Those still show the player and the **Open location** button to play in Finder/QuickTime. (Your lessons folder has both — the `.mp4`s play inline.)

If you hard-reload, you'll see the `.mov` files now show a player frame + Open location, and the `.mp4`s play directly. Want me to add a small "Download" link in the viewer for the non-playable types too?"

"jarvis":"Resize works — the corner drag took the window from 720→880 wide and the dim label updated live to `880×740` (+160w/+120h from the drag). All three issues are fixed and verified:

"jarvis":"I dug through all 237 backend files + the client with four parallel deep-dives. Here's the architecture to its bones, then your four answers.

---

## The architecture, to its bones

**One spine, many limbs.** Every input enters through a *single cognitive door*:

```
HTTP (/api/input/stream, SSE)
  → InputRouter        ── starts with "/" and is a known command? ──► CommandEngine → CommandHandler (deterministic, no LLM)
                       └─ otherwise ──► Orchestrator (the Brain)
                              ├─ load conversation history (last 10 turns from SQLite)
                              ├─ build context (top-5 memories)
                              ├─ Planner.plan()  ── LLM decomposes into a DAG (or heuristic split, or 1 step)
                              ├─ 1 step  ► AgentSelector picks agent → AgentRuntime tool-loop
                              └─ N steps ► PLAN_EXEC pool runs sub-agents in parallel, dependents get upstream outputs
                                            each agent = AgentRuntime loop (≤4 steps) over LanguageModel + ToolRegistry
  → ProviderSwitchingLanguageModel ── picks mock | ollama | openai | claude per call, falls back to mock on error
```

**The layers (34 packages, ~12.8k LOC, 31 test classes):**
- **Cognition:** `input` → `command` → `brain` (Orchestrator, Planner, ContextBuilder) → `agent` (Runtime, Registry of 47 agents, Selector) → `ai` (4 model adapters + 31 tools + ToolRegistry).
- **Capabilities:** `explorer` (files), `memory`, `kb` (RAG), `connectors` (8), `mcp`, `workflow` (cron/webhook/file triggers), `document`, `digest`, `project`, `backup`, `system` (monitor), `local` (macOS).
- **Governance:** `security` (RiskClassifier, PermissionGuard, policy), `approval`, `secrets` (AES vault), `sandbox`, `audit`.
- **Plumbing:** `task`, `conversation`, `notification`, `observability` (run traces + cost), `model` (catalog/router/cost), `plugin` (dynamic JARs), `oauth`, `config`, `error`, 25 REST controllers (~69 endpoints, framed as UI data-plumbing), Flyway V1–V7.
- **Client:** one big `App.tsx` (~1,150 lines, ~20 components), `api.ts` (~50 fetchers through one `req()`), SSE for the brain, polling for status.

The verdict from the code-health pass: **unusually disciplined for its size** — small files, IO-separated adapters, broad tests, real pagination. No large-scale rot. The problems are localized and mostly in two places: **token economics** and **two front-end/threading rough edges**.

---

## Q1 — What to refactor (performance, maintenance, readability)

**HIGH (correctness/safety):**
- **Unbounded thread pool** in `ChatController` (`newCachedThreadPool`) — each SSE stream holds a thread ≤180s during blocking model calls; a burst can exhaust threads. (`InputController` is already bounded — just make Chat match, or retire the duplicate Chat door.)
- **Auth/config errors masked as fake answers** — `ProviderSwitchingLanguageModel` catches *any* RuntimeException and silently falls back to mock. A bad API key → user gets a plausible *mock* answer, not an error. Should surface 4xx/auth failures, only fall back on transient/network errors.
- **Leaked `EventSource` (client)** — `streamInput`'s return is discarded; never closed on unmount or on a new prompt. Rapid prompts/refresh leak connections. Store it in a ref and close it.

**MEDIUM (maintainability):**
- **Three model adapters duplicate ~80%** (build→POST→parse→error-wrap; OpenAI/Ollama share a byte-identical helper). Extract an `AbstractHttpLanguageModel` template — makes the next provider trivial.
- **`Orchestrator` (244 LOC) does too much** — history, persona, routing, DAG executor, cost/observability/audit bookkeeping duplicated across the single- and multi-agent branches. Extract `PlanExecutor` + a `RunRecorder`.
- **KB search loads the whole chunk+document tables into heap every query** (`findAll()` + in-Java cosine). Fine at demo scale, linear degradation. Cap/stream it.
- **`App.tsx` is ~1,150 lines / 20 components.** Split into `windows/`, `FloatingWindow.tsx`, `Sphere.tsx`, `DataView.tsx`, `lib/format.ts`, `lib/errors.ts`; extract a `useResource` hook to kill the repeated "spinner / empty / rows" triad in 7 windows.
- **Client polls `/api/status` every 3s** even though a `subscribeMonitor` SSE already exists and is unused; and the **1s clock re-renders every floating window** (isolate `<Clock/>`).

**LOW:** delete `App.legacy.tsx.bak` (68 KB dead file); merge duplicate `streamChat`/`streamInput`; move 29 inline styles to CSS; add the missing `OrchestratorTest` + `ProviderSwitchingLanguageModelTest`.

---

## Q2 — Features we have / what to add

**Have (real, not stubs):** 31 agent tools · 32 slash commands · 47 agents · 8 connectors (GitHub/Gmail/Calendar/Slack/Notion/Drive/RSS/Maps) · Files Explorer (incl. OS Documents, read/write, media viewers) · Memory · Knowledge Base/RAG · Workflows (manual/cron/webhook/file-change) · Approval Center + Risk + Secrets Vault + Sandbox (opt-in Docker) · OAuth · MCP client · Document gen (PDF/Word/diagram/OCR) · Backup/Restore · System monitor (CPU/mem/disk/network) · Observability (traces, costs, replay) · Plugins (dynamic JAR + marketplace) · 4 model providers · LLM DAG planner · Conversation transcript.

**Worth adding (in rough priority):**
1. **Token governor** (see Q4) — the highest-value addition.
2. **Model-aware routing that's actually honored** — `ModelRouter` picks a model but the adapter ignores it; wire it so cheap tasks use cheap models.
3. **Real agent routing** — replace the keyword `AgentSelector` with model-based routing (kills the last robotic seam).
4. **EMAIL_RECEIVED trigger** + workflow-from-goal generator.
5. **Per-plugin sandboxing / signing** and a remote plugin registry.
6. **Whisper / ElevenLabs** voice (key-gated), GPU telemetry.
7. **Vector index** for KB instead of full-table cosine (scales RAG).

---

## Q3 — Agentic, or robotic?

**Honest answer: the architecture is genuinely agentic; how agentic it *behaves* depends on the model.**

- **Genuinely agentic:** the Brain tool-loop (the model chooses tools, ≤4 steps), the LLM DAG planner (verified live — it decomposed "research → summarize → questions" into a real dependency pipeline), connectors/plugins/memory all exposed as model-chosen tools. On **Ollama/Claude/OpenAI** this is real reasoning, not routing.
- **Robotic by design (correctly):** the 32 slash commands are deterministic — that's the "explicit command" half.
- **The two remaining robotic seams:** (1) the default **mock** model is scripted (looks robotic until you switch providers); (2) **`AgentSelector` routes by keywords**, not reasoning. Everything *after* selection is agentic; the *selection* is a lookup table.

So: not "hard-coded everything" — the spine is a real perceive→plan→act→observe loop. The keyword selector + mock default are the two things that make it *feel* robotic out of the box.

---

## Q4 — Token strategy (so OpenAI/Claude don't burn you)

This is the important one, and the audit found the danger is real. **With the current defaults, one ordinary message can fan out to ~25 model calls**, each re-sending: the persona (~90 words) + agent prompt + 10 full history turns + **~30 uncached tool schemas** — and the default model is **`claude-opus-4-8`, the most expensive in the catalog**. That's the "burning tokens fast" you're worried about, and it's avoidable.

Where the tokens go, worst case for one message:
```
calls = 1 (planner LLM)  +  Σ agents( up to 4 tool-loop steps )
      → single simple: 1–4 · single complex: ~5 · multi-agent: up to 1 + 6×4 = ~25
```
Each call re-sends the full input uncached. `maxTokens=1024` only caps *output*, not the (much larger, repeated) input.

**The fix, in priority order:**

1. **Prompt caching (biggest win, ~0 behavior change).** Mark the system block + tool schemas with Anthropic `cache_control: ephemeral` (and a stable `prompt_cache_key` for OpenAI). They're identical across all 4 loop steps and across a session → steps 2–4 drop to ~10% price. *This alone roughly halves a typical request.*
2. **Route cheap work to cheap models.** Make `ModelRouter`'s choice actually drive the call, and default the planner + simple agents to **Haiku / gpt-4o-mini** (~20× cheaper than Opus), reserving Opus for genuinely hard tasks.
3. **Trim the tool list.** The General agent ships ~30 schemas every step; send a smaller per-agent set (or only on step 1).
4. **Cap history.** Drop `conversationHistoryTurns` 10 → ~4; truncate long turns. (Multiplied across sub-agents, this compounds.)
5. **Skip the planner LLM for simple inputs** more aggressively (raise the length gate / make it opt-in).
6. **A hard token/spend governor** — a per-session and per-day token budget that the Orchestrator checks before each call, with a kill-switch and a visible meter in the HUD. This is the guardrail that makes you *feel safe* turning on a paid key: it can't run away.

Net: caching + cheap-model routing cut the dominant repeated-input cost by roughly an order of magnitude *before* touching behavior; history/tool trims compound it; the governor is the safety net.

---

I kept this to analysis only — no code changed. If you want, I'd suggest tackling it as one focused batch:"

"jarvis":"**Token-safety work landed — the two pieces that directly defuse your "burning tokens fast" fear are done and verified (98 tests green, backend running clean):**

**① Prompt caching + honest errors** (batch 1)
- **Anthropic prompt caching**: the system prompt and the tool-schema array are now marked `cache_control: ephemeral`. Those repeat on every tool-loop step and across a session — caching them turns steps 2-4 into ~10%-price cache reads. **OpenAI** gets a stable `prompt_cache_key` so its auto-cache kicks in.
- **No more masked auth errors**: a bad/expired API key (401/403) now surfaces as a clear error instead of silently answering with the offline mock (so you'll *know* the key is wrong, not get a fake reply).

**② Spend governor + kill-switch** (batch 2) — your safety net
- A **daily token budget** (`jarvis.ai.daily-token-budget`, 0 = unlimited) enforced at the single model chokepoint, **only for paid providers** (Claude/OpenAI) — Ollama and Mock are free and never metered or blocked.
- A **kill-switch**: pause all AI instantly. Over-budget or paused → a friendly "budget reached / AI paused" message, not a runaway.
- Wired into **Settings**: budget input + "AI on / Pause" toggle + live "used today / remaining" line. Verified live: set cap → remaining updates; pause flips; reset works.

So once you add `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, set a daily cap (e.g. 100k) in Settings and it **cannot** run past it.

**Still in the queue (continuing one at a time, as you asked):**
3. Cheap-model routing (planner + simple agents → Haiku/gpt-4o-mini) + history/tool-list trim — *the other half of the token savings*
4. `AbstractHttpLanguageModel` base class (de-dupe the 3 adapters)
5. Safety: bound ChatController's thread pool + fix the leaked client EventSource
6. Client cleanup: split `App.tsx`, use the unused monitor SSE instead of 3s polling, isolate the clock, delete the dead `.bak`

I'll keep going down the list. Want me to continue straight through, or would you rather I pause after each so you can eyeball the changes?"

```
calls = 1 (planner LLM)  +  Σ agents( up to 4 tool-loop steps )
      → single simple: 1–4 · single complex: ~5 · multi-agent: up to 1 + 6×4 = ~25

```

Each call re-sends the full input uncached. `maxTokens=1024` only caps output, not the (much larger, repeated) input.
The fix, in priority order:

1. Prompt caching (biggest win, ~0 behavior change). Mark the system block + tool schemas with Anthropic `cache_control: ephemeral` (and a stable `prompt_cache_key` for OpenAI). They're identical across all 4 loop steps and across a session → steps 2–4 drop to ~10% price. This alone roughly halves a typical request.
2. Route cheap work to cheap models. Make `ModelRouter`'s choice actually drive the call, and default the planner + simple agents to Haiku / gpt-4o-mini (~20× cheaper than Opus), reserving Opus for genuinely hard tasks.
3. Trim the tool list. The General agent ships ~30 schemas every step; send a smaller per-agent set (or only on step 1).
4. Cap history. Drop `conversationHistoryTurns` 10 → ~4; truncate long turns. (Multiplied across sub-agents, this compounds.)
5. Skip the planner LLM for simple inputs more aggressively (raise the length gate / make it opt-in).
6. A hard token/spend governor — a per-session and per-day token budget that the Orchestrator checks before each call, with a kill-switch and a visible meter in the HUD. This is the guardrail that makes you feel safe turning on a paid key: it can't run away.
Net: caching + cheap-model routing cut the dominant repeated-input cost by roughly an order of magnitude before touching behavior; history/tool trims compound it; the governor is the safety net.
I kept this to analysis only — no code changed. If you want, I'd suggest tackling it as one focused batch:


and everything here?
Q4 — Token strategy (so OpenAI/Claude don't burn you)
This is the important one, and the audit found the danger is real. With the current defaults, one ordinary message can fan out to ~25 model calls, each re-sending: the persona (~90 words) + agent prompt + 10 full history turns + ~30 uncached tool schemas — and the default model is `claude-opus-4-8`, the most expensive in the catalog. That's the "burning tokens fast" you're worried about, and it's avoidable.
Where the tokens go, worst case for one message:

```
calls = 1 (planner LLM)  +  Σ agents( up to 4 tool-loop steps )
```

`      → single simple: 1–4 · single complex: ~5 · multi-agent: up to 1 + 6×4 = ~25`
Each call re-sends the full input uncached. `maxTokens=1024` only caps output, not the (much larger, repeated) input.
The fix, in priority order:

1. Prompt caching (biggest win, ~0 behavior change). Mark the system block + tool schemas with Anthropic `cache_control: ephemeral` (and a stable `prompt_cache_key` for OpenAI). They're identical across all 4 loop steps and across a session → steps 2–4 drop to ~10% price. This alone roughly halves a typical request.
2. Route cheap work to cheap models. Make `ModelRouter`'s choice actually drive the call, and default the planner + simple agents to Haiku / gpt-4o-mini (~20× cheaper than Opus), reserving Opus for genuinely hard tasks.
3. Trim the tool list. The General agent ships ~30 schemas every step; send a smaller per-agent set (or only on step 1).
4. Cap history. Drop `conversationHistoryTurns` 10 → ~4; truncate long turns. (Multiplied across sub-agents, this compounds.)
5. Skip the planner LLM for simple inputs more aggressively (raise the length gate / make it opt-in).
6. A hard token/spend governor — a per-session and per-day token budget that the Orchestrator checks before each call, with a kill-switch and a visible meter in the HUD. This is the guardrail that makes you feel safe turning on a paid key: it can't run away.
Net: caching + cheap-model routing cut the dominant repeated-input cost by roughly an order of magnitude before touching behavior; history/tool trims compound it; the governor is the safety net.

How to think about the split (token-wise):
- **Memory store** (capped at 5, always injected, cheap) → put quick always-on facts here (name, timezone, key prefs).
- **About Me profile** (15 pages, fetched on demand via `profile_search`) → the deep reference Jarvis dips into only when relevant.

The only constant cost is the `profile_search` tool's small schema in the tool list each turn — and that's cached on Anthropic/OpenAI, so it's negligible. You + Jarvis still edit the same `about-me.md` (Profile window, Files explorer, or his `update_profile` tool).

The catch: an agent is only as capable as (a) the model behind it and (b) whether it has the right tools + a prompt that actually tells it *how* to do the job.

Your reminders example exposes three concrete gaps, none of which are "the agent is a mock":
1. **`maxSteps = 4`** — the tool loop caps at 4 iterations, so it can write ~3 files then quits. A real app is 20+ files → it *can't* finish, so it leaves a stub/README.
2. **Thin agent prompts** — "Writes backend services" doesn't tell it the *workflow* (put projects under `Projects/<name>`, scaffold backend+client, write real code, not a README).
3. **Model capability** — the 3B local model genuinely can't author a working app no matter the prompt.

So here's the honest, complete answer to your two questions:

- The multi-step planner already decomposes "build X" into backend + client steps routed to those specialists.

**"Can it build 839,484 kinds of apps like you do?"** The *framework* now supports it — but the thing that actually writes correct, complete code **is the model**, exactly like me. A 3B local model **cannot** do this (today's run proved it: wrong agent, prose instead of files). To get real apps built under `Projects/<name>`, you need a capable model:
- **Anthropic Claude or OpenAI** (set the key, switch provider) → genuine multi-file app builds, like you'd expect from me.
- Or a **large local coding model** in Ollama (e.g. `qwen2.5-coder:14b`/`32b`) — far better than `llama3.2:3b` for this.

I deliberately did **not** hard-code any per-app templates — that would be the robotic trap you want to avoid. The agents stay general; they just now *know the build discipline*, and a strong model executes it. 106 tests green; backend live.

"jarvis":"Done. Here's everything:

## 1. Where the agents live — backend ✅
Exactly where they should be. All in `backend/com/jarvis/agent/`:
- `AgentRegistry.java` — the roster (prompts + tool sets + categories)
- `AgentSelector.java` — **the router** (keyword tier → cheap‑LLM tier)
- `AgentRuntime.java` — the model→tool execution loop
- `AgentController.java` (`/api/agents`) just *exposes* them; the client only renders what the backend returns. No agent logic on the client.

## 2. Agents window — categorized + collapsible + total count ✅
Rewrote `AgentsWindow` to mirror the Files explorer:
- **Header at the top:** `35 agents · 10 categories` (live counts).
- **Collapsible category sections** (`▾`/`▸` chevron, count badge), same component style as the jarvis_drive/OS groups.
- Ordered sensibly (General → Development → Research → Data → Files → Connectors → System & Monitoring → Security → Memory → Workflows → Temporary), with friendly labels instead of raw slugs.

Client type-check passes clean. Just hard-reload the UI to see it.

## 3. Can Jarvis run agents async? — Yes ✅
`Orchestrator.executePlan` already does this. A compound request is decomposed into a plan; each sub‑agent runs on a `CompletableFuture` over a **bounded thread pool**. Independent steps run **in parallel**; steps with `dependsOn` are gated so they wait for their upstream outputs (a dependency-aware pipeline), then the answers are merged. Single-step requests just run inline. So: parallel when it can, ordered when it must.

## 4. MCP & Tasks — *not* the same problem as agents
The agents issue was never fake code — it was **model capability** (the 3B model). For these two:
- **Tasks:** fully real — JPA entity + repository + service + SQLite persistence (`com/jarvis/task/`). Not a mock.
- **MCP:** the client is **real JSON-RPC over stdio** — `ProcessBuilder` spawns the server, real `initialize → notifications/initialized → tools/list → tools/call` handshake (`McpClient`/`McpManager`). It's just **dormant by default** because `application.yml` has `servers: []` — no servers configured. So the *logic* is real; it simply has nothing plugged in yet. Whenever you want, I can wire a real MCP server (e.g. the filesystem one already commented out in the yml) and its tools become available to the agent loop automatically.

Want me to enable that example filesystem MCP server so you can see real MCP tools flow through, or leave MCP dormant for now?"

"jarvis":"This is the 3B model failing in three distinct ways at once — and one of them is a **real framework bug** I can fix right now regardless of model. Let me read the agent loop."

"jarvis":"The smoking gun: the 3B model emits tool-call JSON **as plain answer text** instead of using the structured tool API, so the runtime hands that raw JSON straight to you. Let me check how Ollama parses responses."

"jarvis":"Found the exact bug. When the model uses the **native** `tool_calls` field it works — but the 3B model often dumps the tool call as **JSON in the content field**, so `tool_calls` is empty and the runtime shows that raw JSON to you as the "answer." That's a real, model-agnostic bug. I'll salvage text-emitted tool calls in the Ollama adapter."

"jarvis":"I used `mapper()` — let me verify the base class exposes it."

"jarvis":"That transcript actually exposed one **real bug** buried under the model's weakness. Here's the full read and what I fixed.

## What that transcript shows
Three failures, all rooted in `llama3.2:3b`:
1. **Raw JSON leaking as the answer** — `{"name": "calendar_invoke", ...}` shown to you verbatim.
2. **Mis-routing** — "build reminder app" went to Calendar/Debug agents.
3. **Crash** — "I had trouble using a tool."

## The real bug I fixed (model-agnostic) ✅
**#1 was a genuine framework bug, not just the model.** Ollama-class models often emit the tool call as **JSON text in the content field** instead of using the native `tool_calls` channel. The runtime saw "no tool calls," so it printed that JSON to you as the answer.

I added `salvageToolCalls()` to the Ollama adapter: when the content is one (or several stacked) `{"name": ..., "parameters"/"arguments"/"args": {…}}` objects, it converts them into **real tool calls** so the agent loop actually runs the tool. It's careful — strips ```json fences, brace-matches respecting strings, and only triggers when the *whole* message is JSON, so a normal answer that merely mentions JSON is never hijacked. 3 new unit tests cover it.

## The routing gap I closed ✅
"build … app" was falling to the weak LLM router, which guessed Calendar. Added explicit build keywords to the router (`build an app`, `spring boot`, `react app`, `backend`, `frontend`, `rest api`, `implement`…) so build requests now deterministically hit the **code** agent.

## What's still the model (unchanged truth)
Even with the JSON salvage + correct routing, **whether it writes a real reminders app** is the model's job. The 3B will still struggle. The salvage just means it'll *attempt the tool* instead of leaking JSON — but a capable model (Claude/OpenAI, or `qwen2.5-coder:14b`) is what produces the actual code.

All 34 test classes green; backend compiles; client type-checks. Restart the backend to pick up the salvage + routing fixes.

build a reminder app

I can start building a reminder app for you.

**Reminder App Functionality:**

1.  User input: allows users to input reminders with title and content.
2.  Date and time: sets the reminder date and time for each reminder.
3.  Alert options: provides options for alerting users, such as email or in-app notification.
4.  Task status: tracks task status (pending, completed, due).

**Basic Code Structure:**

```python
import datetime

class Reminder:
    def __init__(self, title, content, date_time):
        self.title = title
        self.content = content
        self.date_time = date_time
        self.completed = False

    def mark_as_completed(self):
        self.completed = True

class ReminderApp:
    def __init__(self):
        self.reminders = []

    def add_reminder(self, title, content, date_time):
        new_reminder = Reminder(title, content, date_time)
        self.reminders.append(new_reminder)

    def view_reminders(self):
        for reminder in self.reminders:
            print(f"Title: {reminder.title}")
            print(f"Content: {reminder.content}")
            print(f"Date/Time: {reminder.date_time}")
            print(f"Status: {'Completed' if reminder.completed else 'Pending'}")
            print("")

    def mark_reminder_completed(self, title):
        for reminder in self.reminders:
            if reminder.title == title and not reminder.completed:
                reminder.mark_as_completed()

app = ReminderApp()
```

Composed the answer
I couldn't find a tool to create a full-stack app for reminders with email calendar. However, I can guide you on how to build such an application using popular technologies like Python, Django, React, and Google Calendar API.

**Full-Stack Reminder App:**

1.  Frontend:
    *   Build the UI using ReactJS
    *   Use React Hooks to manage state changes
    *   Utilize Context API for data sharing between components
2.  Backend:
    *   Choose a Python framework like Django
    *   Create APIs for user authentication, reminder creation, and calendar synchronization
    *   Use the Google Calendar API for integration with email calendar
3.  Database:
    *   Design a database schema to store reminders, users' information, and calendar data
4.  Email Integration:
    *   Use Gmail's SMTP server for sending emails
    *   Utilize the Gmail API for calendar synchronization

**Example Code Structure:**

```python
# Frontend (ReactJS)
import React, { useState } from 'react';
import ReactDOM from 'react-dom';

// Backend (Django)
from django.http import HttpResponse
from django.shortcuts import render
from django.views.generic import TemplateView
from googleapiclient.discovery import build

# Database Model
class Reminder(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=255)
    content = models.TextField()
    date_time = models.DateTimeField()

class Calendar:
    def __init__(self, email, password):
        self.email = email
        self.password = password
        self.calendar_service = build('calendar', 'v3')

# Email Integration
from googleapiclient.discovery import build

class GmailAPI:
    def __init__(self, email, password):
        self.email = email
        self.password = password
        self.smtp_server = None

    def sync_calendar(self):
        # Initialize SMTP server
        self.smtp_server = smtplib.SMTP('smtp.gmail.com', 587)
        self.smtp_server.starttls()
        self.smtp_server.login(self.email, self.password)

        # Create a new event in the calendar
        event = {
            'summary': 'Reminder',
            'description': 'This is a reminder',
            'start': {'date': '2024-01-01T12:00:00', 'timezoneOffset': -5},
            'end': {'date': '2024-01-01T13:00:00', 'timezoneOffset': -5}
        }
        self.calendar_service.events().insert(calendarId='primary', body=event).execute()
```

This is a basic example of how you can build a full-stack reminder app with email calendar integration. You'll need to handle authentication, authorization, and error handling in your production application.

## What I fixed (real bugs — verified & tested)

"jarvis":"Let me read the actual ModelRouter and see whether it's wired into the live agent path."

"jarvis":"Now the critical question — does the Orchestrator actually *use* the routed model, or just display it? Let me read that path."

"jarvis":"Let me confirm the two remaining pieces: how the live model is actually chosen (ProviderSwitching) and what the catalog considers "available.""

"jarvis":"Read the whole path. Here's the precise, honest answer.

## Short answer
**Partially.** The *scaffolding and the decision algorithm* exist and are real — but the "pick the best model for this task" decision is **computed and displayed, not actually applied** to the main answer. The only model-per-task decision that's truly wired end-to-end is the **cheap-model override for planning/routing**.

## What actually exists (and is real)
- **`ModelRouter`** (`model/ModelRouter.java`) — a genuine scoring algorithm. Given a `RoutingPreference` it picks from available models:
  - `QUALITY` → highest `quality` score, tie-break cheaper
  - `CHEAP` → lowest blended cost
  - `BALANCED` → `quality − cost×20 − latency/2000 + localBonus`
  - `PRIVATE` → filters to **local-only** models first (nothing leaves the machine)
- **`ModelCatalog`** — model descriptors with `quality`, `costInputPer1k`, `costOutputPer1k`, `latencyMs`, `local` flags.
- **`CostCalculator`** + observability records the model id, tokens, and $ cost per run.
- **Cheap-model override (the one that genuinely works):** the lightweight **planner** and **agent-router** calls run on a cheap model via `generate(messages, tools, plannerModel()/routerModel())`, which overrides the request body's `"model"` key (`AbstractHttpLanguageModel` line 53-54). So Jarvis really does spend `claude-3-5-haiku` / `gpt-4o-mini` on the cheap meta-reasoning and saves the expensive model for the actual work. That's real task-aware cost routing.

## The three gaps (why it's not truly per-task yet)
1. **`route(taskType)` ignores its argument.** `public ModelDescriptor route(String taskType){ return choose(catalog.available(), props.getPrivacy()); }` — it applies **one global preference** to everything. There's no "code task → high-quality model, quick chat → cheap model" logic. The `taskType`/agent slug is passed in (`Orchestrator` line 106) but unused.
2. **The chosen model isn't executed.** `Orchestrator` computes `model = modelRouter.route(...)`, emits the "Routed to model X" step, and uses it for **cost + observability only**. The agent then runs via `runtime.run(...)` → `model.generate(messages, tools)` with **no override** → it always uses whatever single provider `jarvis.ai.provider` points at. So the router's pick is **advisory/cosmetic** for the real answer.
3. **One global provider, not per-call.** `ProviderSwitchingLanguageModel.active()` resolves a *single* provider from settings; there's no switching OpenAI↔Claude↔Ollama per task. You pick the provider in Settings and everything goes there.

## So, agentic model selection?
The *intent* and the *math* are there, plus real cheap-routing for meta-calls — but the headline "best model per task for quality/perf/tokens" is **designed, not wired**. To make it genuinely agentic I'd need to:
- **(a)** make `route()` actually reason about the task/agent — e.g. build/code/debug → highest-quality (Claude Opus/Sonnet or qwen-coder); quick Q&A/summarize → cheap (haiku/gpt-4o-mini/local); privacy-tagged → force local Ollama; and respect the token budget (downshift when near the cap).
- **(b)** thread the chosen model's **provider + id** into `AgentRuntime.generate(...)` so the call actually runs on that model — i.e. per-call provider switching, not just the global setting.

That's a real, self-contained feature (a few files: `ModelRouter`, `Orchestrator`/`AgentRuntime` plumbing, and per-call provider resolution in `ProviderSwitchingLanguageModel`). Want me to build it — make per-task model selection actually drive execution — or just leave the answer here for now?"

## How it works (general, not micro-specific)
1. **Tools self-declare side effects** — added a `mutates()` flag to the `Tool` interface; the 13 action tools (write_file, connector, memory_write, backup_create, create_pdf/docx, screenshot, …) return `true`, read-only tools stay `false`. So this applies to *any* action — files, email, memory, backups — not just builds.
2. **The runtime tracks reality** — per turn it knows whether the agent *can* act, whether it called tools, and whether any side-effecting action actually **succeeded** (error results don't count).
3. **Trigger** — an action-capable agent that used tools, landed **zero** successful actions, *and* whose reply claims it completed something (generic completion-language detector).
4. **Correction is agentic-first, robust-fallback** — it first asks the model to reconcile the reply against the real tool results and rewrite honestly. But I **validate** that rewrite: the weak 3B verifier literally echoed the prompt and dumped code (`REPLY: … ```java`), so any malformed/blank/code-laden output is rejected and replaced with a deterministic honest banner: *"⚠️ I didn't actually complete that — no action succeeded this turn… treat the details below as a plan, not a result."* Capable models get the clean rewrite; weak models get the reliable banner; **you're never told a fabricated success.**

## Verified
- **3 new unit tests:** fires on a fabricated success → banner; silent when a write truly succeeds; silent when nothing was claimed. **37 test classes, all green.**
- **Live:** re-ran "create fullstack app…" — this time the 3B *actually wrote 9 files* (5 backend `.java` + 4 client `.tsx`), so the guard **correctly stayed silent** because the answer was truthful.

"jarvis":"Done — per-task model selection is built, wired into execution, tested, and live.

## What it does now (real, not advisory)
The Model Router picks a model **per task** and that model **actually runs** the call — switching provider mid-session when it helps.

**The decision (in `ModelRouter.route(agentSlug)`):**
- **Explicit preference wins globally** — if you set `QUALITY` / `CHEAP` / `PRIVATE` in settings, that's honored for everything.
- **Default (`BALANCED`) → per-task tier decides:**
  - **HEAVY** (code, backend, frontend, debug, review, data, research…) → **highest-quality** model (e.g. Claude Opus).
  - **LIGHT** (system status, files, calendar, email, memory…) → **cheapest** capable model (free local Ollama).
  - **STANDARD** (general) → a blended quality/cost/latency score.
- **Budget-aware:** if you're paused or ≥80% of the daily token cap, it **conserves** — forces free local / cheapest.
- **Never routes real work to the mock** when a real model exists; **PRIVATE** keeps everything on-device.

**The execution (the part that was missing):**
- `ProviderSwitchingLanguageModel.generateOn(provider, modelId, …)` now resolves the adapter **for that provider** per call (Ollama / OpenAI / Anthropic), with the same metering + auth-error + fallback safety. So a single turn can run a heavy sub-task on Claude and a light one on local Ollama.
- `AgentRuntime` runs on the chosen model; `Orchestrator` routes the single-step path **and each sub-agent in a plan** independently (the step trace shows the chosen model id).

## Verified
- **6 new tests** (tiers, conserve, slug→tier mapping, cross-provider `route()` mock-avoidance, heavy→opus vs light→ollama with a key) + the existing router tests still pass. **40 test classes green.**
- **Live:** "what is my cpu status" → System Agent (LIGHT) → routed to and executed on `llama3.2:3b`. `/api/models` now lists all three providers with availability/cost/quality.

## One honest note
Right now you only have **Ollama** configured (no API keys), so all real work correctly goes to Ollama — the cross-provider tiering (heavy→Claude/OpenAI, light→local) only becomes *visible* once you add an OpenAI or Anthropic key in Settings. The moment you do, heavy tasks will start routing to the strong cloud model and light ones will stay local/free automatically. Backend restarted and healthy (`:8088`); client on `localhost:5173`."

Browsed the web, used a tool

Yes — and that's a fair comparison; both projects are called Jarvis, both are personal AI command centers, both use registries for extensibility, both use SQLite. But OpenJarvis is a Python/CLI/headless thing with no UI, while yours is a Mac-native TypeScript stack with the HUD as the whole point. So the architectures rhyme but optimize for different jobs.
Where they're parallel:
* Capability/tool registry with typed schemas
* SQLite for persistence (you both even use FTS5)
* Slash-command-ish entry points (theirs is CLI subcommands, yours is / in chat)
* LLM-driven intent resolution
* Approval/sandbox gates before risky execution
Where OpenJarvis genuinely does things you could steal:
1. EventBus with typed events — they have a thread-safe in-process pub/sub (INFERENCE_START/END, TOOL_CALL_*, MEMORY_*, SECURITY_*). You have correlation logging (R3) but no formal event bus. A typed bus would clean up your notification fanout + audit + bell-badge plumbing — right now each of those subscribes via direct DB writes.
2. OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint — they expose this so any OpenAI-SDK client (Cursor, Aider, lots of CLIs) can drive Jarvis transparently. You'd get a huge ecosystem for ~200 LOC of route + chunked SSE that maps to your existing chat pipeline.
3. Hybrid RRF retrieval (FAISS + BM25 + ColBERTv2 fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion). You have FTS5 + embeddings (R6.5) but not fused — RRF on top of your existing two signals is a known retrieval-quality win and costs almost nothing.
4. Container-sandboxed agent execution (SandboxedAgent runs in Docker/Podman, ContainerRunner manages lifecycle). For your terminal-task, install-app, write-files capabilities — anything that touches shell or filesystem — wrapping execution in a per-run Docker container is a real security boundary that your current dryrun+approval pattern can't provide.
5. Multiple agent strategies as first-class — OrchestratorAgent (tool-calling loop), NativeReActAgent (Thought/Action/Observation), NativeOpenHandsAgent (CodeAct-style), RLMAgent (recursive LM with persistent REPL), MonitorOperativeAgent (long-horizon). You have one planner (Slice 3). For long-running things — file refactoring, deep research, code generation — a CodeAct or ReAct loop with persistent state would beat your current single-shot planner.
6. Trace-driven policy learning — TraceAnalyzer → TraceDrivenPolicy → router. They record every interaction and learn which model/agent/tool was successful for similar queries, then auto-route future requests. You have audit logs (R12 R1.3, R12 audit replay) but they're not feeding back into the router/model picker. This is direct cost savings: cheap model first, escalate only on bad outputs.
7. Skills loadable from GitHub at runtime — SkillManager discovers from skills_dir and can pull from GitHub. You currently in-tree every capability. A /install <gh:owner/repo> capability store would let users add abilities without you cutting a release.
8. CLI surface (jarvis ask, jarvis chat, jarvis serve, 40+ subcommands). Your interaction surface is entirely the HUD — which is your strength, but useless over SSH, in cron jobs, or from another machine on the LAN. A small jarvis CLI that proxies to your existing Express API would unlock headless usage.
Where you do things they don't:
* The HUD itself (particle sphere, floating windows, reactive voice loop) — they have no UI at all, just CLI + HTTP API + messaging channels
* Mac-native integrations (file index, open-application with iframe/popup fallback, browser-action via Playwright, calendar, mail)
* Streaming chat with token deltas in the UI (Phase D #7)
* Push approvals to phone (Phase C #6)
* Bell + notification center for live user feedback
* Real-time mic-reactive visuals
If I were ranking what to steal, in order:
Rank	Item	Effort	Impact
1	OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions shim	Low	High — unlocks ecosystem
2	Hybrid RRF retrieval over your FTS5 + embeddings	Low	Medium — better recall
3	Typed in-process EventBus	Medium	Medium — cleans up notification/audit plumbing
4	Container sandbox for shell/file capabilities	Medium	High — real security boundary
5	Trace-driven model routing	Medium-High	High — real $ savings
6	CLI surface	Low-Medium	Medium — headless/ssh access
7	Agent-strategy enum (ReAct / CodeAct / single-shot)	High	Medium — only matters for long-horizon work
8	GitHub skills loader	High	Low — until you have an ecosystem to load
Want me to spin up one of these as a task? Item 1 (OpenAI-compatible endpoint) is the highest-leverage cheap win — about a day's work and your Jarvis suddenly speaks the lingua franca of AI tooling.

"jarvis":"Read all of it. Most of that dump is **old-version notes** (the `/automations`, `/connectors`, `/rollback`, sleep-mode pages, "View all/Manage" dead buttons, the TypeScript/Express stack, `agents` vs `agents-v2`) — none of that exists in our current build, so there's nothing to "fix" there. Our stack is already organized the way you describe (`com.jarvis.brain`, `agent`, `ai`, `mcp`, `explorer`, `model`…, logic in the backend, no v2 duplication). So I filtered for **ideas**, not chores.

Here's what genuinely catches my eye — and the throughline is your own core principle: **Jarvis should discover its own capabilities and pick the right one.** Almost everything good in that pile is a variation of that.

## 🎯 The one that matters most — capability self-discovery
Your "weather in Tel Aviv → I can't do that" is *the* signal. The General agent already *has* `web_search` and its prompt already says "use it for current facts" — but the model (esp. 3B) doesn't reliably connect "weather" → "I have a search tool." You're right that we can't hand-cover every phrasing.

The agentic fix (general, zero hard-coded scenarios): a **capability-reflection retry**. When the model is about to *refuse* ("I can't / I don't have access / I'm unable"), don't accept it — re-prompt once with an explicit list of its own tools: *"You said you can't. Here are your tools: [web_search, calculate, …]. Which one accomplishes this? If one fits, use it."* That's self-reflection over its own capabilities, works for weather/prices/news/math/anything, and helps even mid models. This is the single most "Jarvis figures it out himself" change on the table.

## 🗣️ The voice-UX cluster (your daily pain, headless/voice-first)
These three from your notes are concrete and high-value, and they're mostly client + one small backend field:
1. **Spoken = short, chat = full.** For search/explain answers, the model returns a 1–2 sentence `spokenSummary` + the full text. Voice reads the summary; the window shows everything.
2. **"Continue / keep going / tell me more"** → then it speaks the full version. (Agentic: it's just a follow-up intent, no new hard-coding.)
3. **Barge-in / interrupt** — say anything (or a wake word) and TTS stops immediately. Plus an **"want me to read the long version?"** gate before reading anything long.

## 🔌 Your MCP/agents question — yes, this is the big leverage
You asked: *can we use MCPs + our agents to offload the work Jarvis does, instead of hand-coding capabilities?* **Exactly right, and it's the most agentic path.** We already have a **real MCP client** (JSON-RPC over stdio) — it's just dormant (`servers: []`). Turning on MCP servers (filesystem, **Playwright/browser**, fetch, etc.) gives Jarvis whole new capability sets that the agent loop auto-discovers and uses — **no raw plan code from us per capability.** That's the "add abilities without cutting a release" idea from the dump, done properly. With your Claude Code $100 sub, browser-automation via MCP is the single biggest capability unlock.

## 💡 Worth stealing later (ranked), reframed agentic
- **Trace-driven self-learning router** — record which model/agent/tool actually succeeded, then auto-route similar future requests (cheap model first, escalate only on failure). This is literally "self-learning" + real token savings, and it builds directly on the per-task router we just shipped. *High impact, medium effort.*
- **ReAct/CodeAct agent loop** for big tasks — a Thought→Action→Observation loop with reflection beats single-shot for "split a big task into chunks." We have a planner; this upgrades the *execution* of each chunk. *Medium.*
- **OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` shim** — lets Cursor/Aider/any tool drive Jarvis. Cheap, but only matters if you want external clients. *Low effort, niche for you.*
- **Hybrid RRF retrieval** over our existing KB embeddings + keyword — cheap recall quality win. *Low.*
- Skip for now: EventBus (nice cleanup, low user value), CLI surface (you drive by voice), container sandbox (we already have `SandboxService` with Docker `--network none`), GitHub skills loader (we already have the Plugin SDK).

## Don't-touch / already-have
Container sandbox ✅ (SandboxService), plugin/skill loading ✅ (Plugin SDK), cost dashboard ✅ (token dashboard), streaming ✅ (SSE), per-task model routing ✅ (just built). The architecture cleanup you describe is essentially already true here.

My honest ranking for "makes Jarvis feel agentic & self-directed": **capability self-discovery → voice UX → MCP servers → trace-learning router.**

Which do you want me to build first? (I'll do it agentic + backend-heavy, one at a time.)"

"jarvis":"No problem — that question dialog covered my analysis. I won't pop another one. My full read is right above this (the "catches the eye" breakdown). Take your time reading it.

When you're ready, just tell me which to build first — here are the four, shortest form:

1. **Capability self-discovery** — Jarvis reflects over its own tools before saying "I can't" (the general fix for "weather in Tel Aviv"). *My #1 pick — it's the heart of your philosophy.*
2. **Voice UX cluster** — short spoken summary / full in chat, "continue" for more, barge-in to interrupt, ask-before-reading-long.

"shay":"The agentic fix (general, zero hard-coded scenarios): a capability-reflection retry. When the model is about to refuse ("I can't / I don't have access / I'm unable"), don't accept it — re-prompt once with an explicit list of its own tools: "You said you can't. Here are your tools: [web_search, calculate, …]. Which one accomplishes this? If one fits, use it." That's self-reflection over its own capabilities, works for weather/prices/news/math/anything, and helps even mid models. This is the single most "Jarvis figures it out himself" change on the table.

Spoken = short, chat = full. For search/explain answers, the model returns a 1–2 sentence spokenSummary + the full text. Voice reads the summary; the window shows everything.
"Continue / keep going / tell me more" → then it speaks the full version. (Agentic: it's just a follow-up intent, no new hard-coding.)
Barge-in / interrupt — say anything (or a wake word) and TTS stops immediately. Plus an "want me to read the long version?" gate before reading anything long.

Trace-driven self-learning router — record which model/agent/tool actually succeeded, then auto-route similar future requests (cheap model first, escalate only on failure). ## ✅ Built

**1. Capability self-discovery (reflection-retry)** — *the core philosophy fix.*
When Jarvis's answer is a **refusal** ("I can't / I don't have access / no real-time…") but the agent actually has tools, the runtime injects **one** nudge listing its own capabilities — *"you have [web_search, calculate, …]; if any can do this, call it now"* — emits a visible **"Re-checking my own capabilities"** step, and lets it try again. General, zero per-query hard-coding; works for weather, prices, news, math, files. Tested (reflects→uses a tool; no-tools→no reflect). Live test: "weather in Tel Aviv" → went straight to `web_search` (the model cooperated; reflection is the safety net for when it doesn't).

**3. Voice UX (client).**
- **Spoken = short, chat = full:** long answers speak only the first 1–2 sentences + *"say 'continue' to hear the rest"*; the full text stays in the window.
- **"Continue / keep going / tell me more / elaborate"** → speaks the full version, handled client-side (no AI round trip).
- **Barge-in:** any new input or mic press calls `speechSynthesis.cancel()` — Jarvis stops mid-sentence.

## ⏸️ Deferred — and why (your one requirement)

**2. Trace-driven self-learning router — not built yet, on purpose.** A router that *learns which model to pick* has **nothing to learn from while you're on a single provider** — every real task goes to Ollama, so there's no model-choice to optimize from feedback. The substrate is ready (we persist agent/model/status per run); I'll build it the moment you add an OpenAI/Anthropic key, when it actually has a decision to improve. Building it now = a feedback loop with no signal + needless risk to the router we just shipped.

**ReAct/CodeAct loop — deferred.** Our runtime is *already* a tool-calling (ReAct-equivalent) loop, and the DAG planner already splits big tasks. A separate strategy is a large, higher-risk rewrite whose payoff only appears on a strong model. Revisit when you switch models.

Jarvis — Local AI OS for Mac mini M1
What it is
A local-first AI command center that runs on your Mac (designed for an M1 mini) and is reachable from your laptop, iPhone, or any device on the same network. You talk to it by voice or text; it executes capabilities — opening apps, installing software, writing files, searching the web, scaffolding code projects, taking screenshots, managing your calendar — and everything it does lives in a discoverable ~/Documents/Jarvis_exports/ folder.
Critical design choice: it works without sharing any API keys. There's no required LLM. Optional integrations (Google OAuth, Brave Search, etc.) are layered on top — you opt in by pasting credentials. The default install uses only free / native / public sources.
Core architecture (1-minute version)
Piece	What it does
Backend (Node + Express + SQLite, port 3000)	Capability runtime, workflow engine, audit log, OAuth flows, file index, scheduler
Frontend (Next.js + Tailwind, port 3001)	Dashboard, voice bar, History, Connectors, Settings, App Builder, Sleep mode
Capability Registry	Every action (open-application, install-app, write-files, take-screenshot…) is a Capability with input schema, risk level, rollback recipe, audit linkage
Execution Runtime	Single choke-point — every voice/chat/chip/agent invocation goes through it (policy gate → restrictions check → run → audit → notification → rollback registration)
Restrictions	Hard-blocks 20 patterns across network-settings, risky-shell, finance-external — defense in depth
Workflows	Multi-step pipelines (app generator does Plan → Approve → Scaffold → Code → QA → Deliver)
Connectors	MCP-style external integrations (GitHub, Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Tasks, Notion, Slack, Discord, Skype)
Jarvis_exports tree	Every file Jarvis creates lands here, organized into subfolders
Test status: 296 sandbox tests passing across 21 suites.

What you can do today

Web Speech API voice round-trip (mic → STT → orchestrator → reply → TTS)	✅	Works in Chrome/Safari/Edge; type-fallback when mic unavailable
Sticky bottom voice bar on every page	✅	Cmd-K focus, ESC cancel
Sleep-mode wake-word loop	✅	Says "wake up Jarvis" / "hey Jarvis" / "ok Jarvis" — robust auto-restart, mic-denial graceful
Chat endpoint with planner-routed capabilities	✅	POST /api/chat
Voice/chat parity for every chip	✅	Anything you can click, you can say
✅ Done — Capabilities (the things Jarvis can DO)

LLM integration	You explicitly opted out of sharing an API key. Without one, chat replies fall back to a templated message when no capability matches. Local Ollama integration is the obvious follow-on.
Image / video generation	Needs a model (diffusion or similar). Same reason as LLM.
Real Tailscale / external VPN	Mentioned in Settings as "Coming soon".
