How to get started (fresh checkout)










# 1. install deps
cd backend  && npm install
cd ../frontend && npm install

# 2. boot both
cd backend  && npm run dev   # serves port 3000
cd ../frontend && npm run dev   # serves port 3001

# 3. open
open http://localhost:3001/dashboard
From your iPhone on the same Wi-Fi: http://<mac-ip>:3001/dashboard.
Optional one-time setups
* Google OAuth (unlocks real Gmail/Calendar/Drive/Tasks) — see backend/docs/OAUTH_SETUP.md. 10 minutes in Google Cloud Console + paste 2 env vars.
* Auth lockdown — set JARVIS_REQUIRE_AUTH=true in backend/.env. After that the dashboard requires login.
* File index — first crawl happens 60s after boot, takes ~5s for ~2k files. Re-crawl every 4h by default, tunable via JARVIS_INDEX_REBUILD_HOURS.

What it looks like in your hands
* Voice: Tap the mic in the bottom bar (any page) → say "take a screenshot and save it as a pdf called report" → backend decomposes into two child capabilities, you see the live timeline as each completes, both files land in Jarvis_exports/, bell pings twice.
* Sleep: Idle for 5 minutes → screen dims to the orb + clock → say "hey Jarvis" → back to dashboard.
* Find a doc: Type into the Find a doc chip → ranked hits from 2,159 indexed files appear with highlighted snippets → click any → opens in Finder.
* Research the web: Type "what is jarvis ai" into the Research chip → AI Overview paragraph + 8 source chips from Wikipedia + HackerNews + Stack Overflow + GitHub appear instantly.


JARVIS
JUST · A · RATHER · VERY · INTELLIGENT · SYSTEM
Local-first personal AI on your Mac. Voice, chat, a sandboxed action surface, a memory that persists, and a HUD that never tries to replace your workflow — just sit alongside it.
WHAT JARVIS IS
Jarvis is a local-first personal AI operating system running on your Mac. It listens to your voice or text, decides what to do, runs the action through a vetted capability, and reports back — all without your data leaving the machine unless you've explicitly enabled a cloud model provider.
Think of it as your own private assistant with three things most cloud AIs don't have: it sees your filesystem, it can act on your behalf (open apps, send mail, run terminal commands), and it keeps a persistent memory of you across sessions.
THE SIX LAYERS
1 · BRAIN
Everything LLM-shaped lives under backend/src/brain/ (R13). Provider routing (Ollama / OpenAI / Anthropic with privacy + latency + complexity decision table), LRU cache, pricing, trace-driven outcomes, intent resolution (regex first, LLM fallback), multi-step planner, chat reply generator, memory-aware prompt context.
2 · CAPABILITIES
Smallest unit of executable functionality (28 of them). Each capability declares its risk level, rollback recipe, and dryrun behaviour. Voice / chat / dashboard all go through the same registry — no special paths.
3 · AGENTS
Stateful multi-turn workers (11 of them). Orchestrator plans a DAG, coders generate 20–50 files, validator runs npm install + tsc, tester runs Playwright, devops makes Dockerfiles, publisher deploys behind a typed-confirm gate. Agents call capabilities; capabilities never call agents.
4 · MCPS (INTEGRATIONS)
Adapters to external systems: Gmail, Calendar, Drive, GitHub, Notion, Slack, Discord, plus local services (file index, network, audio, STT, TTS, wake-word, chat history). All share retry / circuit-breaker / rate-limit infrastructure.
5 · ORCHESTRATOR
Top-level dispatch: takes a message, asks the brain to classify it (single capability vs multi-step plan vs freeform chat), routes accordingly. Coexists with the older workflows/ engine which still drives create-project + scheduled workflows.
6 · HUD (THIS UI)
The interface you're looking at. Floating windows, voice sphere, slash-command registry, action queue (left rail), live status (bottom-right), composite history (top-right bell).
HOW TO USE IT
* Just say "Jarvis." Wake-word listening is on by default (A33). Say "Jarvis open settings" in one breath, OR say "Jarvis" → pause → "what's the weather" — either works. The HUD whispers "Yes?" then "Awaiting command…" while it listens.
* Type or talk into the chat bar. "Open Spotify", "calculate 2+3*4", "what did I commit yesterday", "remind me to call Sarah at 3pm".
* Voice slash commands (A32). Web Speech can't transcribe the literal "/" — so saying "slash help", "open settings", "show me the dashboard", "go to history", or "launch jarvis explorer" all map to their slash equivalents automatically. The list of triggers: "slash X", "open X", "open the X", "show X", "show me X", "go to X", "navigate to X", "launch X".
* Click the sphere. Arms wake-word awaiting-command mode (A34) without opening a second mic stream — same as saying "Jarvis." Safe to click any time.
* Slash commands. Type /help or /about in the prompt bar. There are 23 registered — see the list below.
* Approvals. Capabilities marked confirm-risk (install-app, terminal commands, mail send) park in the left rail's Notification Center. You approve / reject with one click; the runtime never proceeds without consent.
* Floating windows. Every workspace (settings / files / memory / usage / chat history / etc) lives in a draggable, resizable floating window so you can keep the HUD in view.
SLASH COMMANDS
/help
Open the floating help window with every feature + slash command
/settings
Open the floating Settings window (voice, file index, models, push approvals, model usage, memory)
/chat
Open the floating Chat window — full live conversation
/history
Open the floating Multi-step history window (composite prompts + sub-step trees)
/clear
Wipe the current chat conversation (does not affect audit history)
/tasks
Open the floating Tasks window (heartbeat-scheduled jobs + active runs + completed history)
/jarvis_explorer
Open the Jarvis Exports tree (screenshots, PDFs, generated projects, installs)
/today
Open the Jarvis Today digest — daily / weekly counts, top capabilities, and recent highlights
/integrations
Open the Integrations window (Gmail / Calendar / Drive / Notion / Tasks / GitHub OAuth)
/automations
Open the Automations window (scheduled jobs + recurring tasks)
/files
Open the File Explorer — tree of Documents, Desktop, Downloads, Jarvis exports, and project folders
/capabilities
Open the Capability sandbox — list every registered capability + run any of them with custom JSON args
/about
Open the About window — what Jarvis is, the four layers, every slash command, safety model, where data lives, how to extend
/usage
Open the Usage window — calls / tokens / cost rolled up by provider and capability over 24h / 7d / 30d
/memory
Open the Memory window — list/delete trusted facts, accept or reject auto-extracted proposals, and run a fresh scan over recent chats
/find
Search persisted chat history — full-text across every message Jarvis has saved
/digest
Run the morning digest on-demand — yesterday's activity, today's calendar, upcoming tasks, pinned memory
/diagram
Generate a diagram from a description
/calc
Evaluate a math expression
/image
Generate an image from a prompt
/build
Kick off the multi-agent orchestrator to build an app from a prompt
/dashboard
Open the unified workbench — agent runs, scheduled jobs, generated projects
/agents
Open the agent roster — "meet the team" view with each agent's role + capabilities + a Run button
THE THREE STATUS SURFACES
* Bottom-right panel: live "what is Jarvis doing right now" — provider dots, currently-running capability, next scheduled job. Passive status.
* Top-right bell: composite multi-step task history. Click to drill into a chain of runs.
* Left rail (Notification Center): action queue — approvals, memory proposals, unread inbox. Hover the left edge to slide it out.
SAFETY MODEL
* Risk levels. Every capability declares safe / confirm / blocked. Confirm-risk parks for approval; blocked needs an explicit policy override.
* EXECUTE typed-confirm. Danger-risk terminal commands require the user to literally type "EXECUTE" before they run.
* Restrictions. A configurable JSON list (data/restrictions.json) hard-blocks network-settings tampering, risky shell patterns, and finance flows. Edit via the Settings window or by hand.
* Audit log. Every run lands in audit_log with the input, output, status, and rollback ref. Visible via the bell + the history window.
* Rollback. Capabilities that mutate state declare a rollback recipe (delete created files, restore snapshot, etc).
WHERE YOUR DATA LIVES
* ~/Documents/Jarvis_exports/ — every file Jarvis creates (PDFs, images, scaffolded projects, screenshots).
* backend/data/jarvis.db — SQLite. Auto-backups before every migration in backend/data/backups/.
* backend/data/file-index.db — FTS5 index over your Documents / Desktop. Optional embeddings via Ollama.
* backend/data/oauth-tokens/ — per-provider OAuth tokens (chmod 600). Gmail / Calendar / Drive / Notion / Microsoft.
* backend/config/jarvis.toml — single source of truth for every non-secret setting. Secrets via env.
CONFIGURATION
Edit backend/config/jarvis.toml for non-secret tunables — ports, retention, MCP timeouts, scheduler cadence, allowed file-explorer roots. Boot log prints "config loaded — N settings (default:X, toml:Y, env:Z)" so you can always see which layer produced a value.
Secrets stay in env (Docker / shell): OPENAI_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, JWT_SECRET, JARVIS_PUSH_TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN, JARVIS_EMAIL_PASSWORD, etc.
EXTENDING JARVIS
* New capability: drop a folder under backend/src/capabilities/<your-id>/, declare the Zod input/output schema + dryrunBehaviour, register in core/capability-registry/index.ts. The lint script (npm run lint:capabilities) enforces the contract.
* New MCP: implement the Mcp interface under backend/src/mcps/<id>-mcp/. Inherits retry + circuit-breaker from mcps/shared/http.ts for free.
* New slash command: one entry in frontend/lib/slash-commands.ts. Help docs sync automatically.
THE HISTORY THAT SHIPPED
Jarvis has gone through 12 refactor phases (R0 through R12) on top of the original feature phases. The big structural moves:
* R0–R4: typed DB, migrations, error envelope, correlation IDs, MCP resilience.
* R5: capability contract + dryrun lint.
* R6: LRU model cache, SQL aggregation, combined /api/hud/tick.
* R7: floating-windows store, useMutation, React.memo, ErrorBoundary.
* R8: apiFetch with retry / dedup / 401 refresh.
* R9–R10: test hygiene + live verification fixes (Tailscale CIDR bug, TTS race, hydration mismatch).
* R11: config consolidation (jarvis.toml), calculator + diagram capabilities, file editor (CodeMirror), memory CRUD + extractor, Notification Center, SMTP transport, usage dashboard, Docker watchdog.
* R12: live-feedback polish — single/double-click trigger, image+video preview, commas in calculator, action-queue scoping, sphere clip, /about, in-Jarvis webview, sphere upgrade.
* R12.17–R12.21: sphere v6 — replaced the Fibonacci shell + wireframe edges with a volumetric particle cloud (1500 dots, centre-bias, wispy radial streaks), Iron-Man HUD ring system (dashed inner ring with [ ] brackets at top + bottom, dotted outer ring with 36 outward turret spikes, sweep pip), removed legacy CSS chrome (conic-gradient sweep + ring-marker dots that produced the "rectangle artifact"), scaled the canvas to fill the wrapper.
* Agent system foundation (A0–A2): typed AgentSpec contract mirroring the capability registry — input/output zod schemas, allowedCapabilities + allowedMcps allow-lists enforced at call time, dryrunBehaviour, agent lint (npm run lint:agents). Runs persist to agent_runs + agent_run_steps (migration 0007). Live SSE at /api/v2/agents/runs/:id/stream. Folder rename: legacy Phase 5 code moved to workflows/app-builder-roles/; new system lives under src/agents/.
* Ten built-in agents (A3–A8, A18): 📣 notifier (push/email fanout), 🔬 researcher (web-research + synthesis), 📋 requirements-writer (asks clarifying questions via ctx.ask()), 🧭 orchestrator (LLM plans a DAG of sub-agent calls), ⚙️ backend-coder + 🎨 frontend-coder (multi-pass scaffold 20–50 files), 🧪 tester (Playwright via browser-action), 🛠️ devops (Dockerfile/compose/fly.toml/vercel.json), 🚀 publisher (deploy CLI gated by typed-confirm DEPLOY), ✅ validator (npm install + tsc --noEmit + lint + tests).
* Multi-pass coders (A13): each coder runs an architect pass to plan 20–50 files, then fires one LLM call per file in parallel batches of 5. Produces real working apps, not skeletons.
* HUD surfaces (A4, A11, A12, A19, A26, A28): /dashboard = unified workbench with two sections (📦 Projects · ⏱ Jobs). Agent runs only appear UNDER their parent project — selecting a project shows its scoped runs; clicking a run expands the live step tree + structured output INLINE beneath the row (no popup). /agents = "meet the team" roster — cards with emoji, role, impl pill, risk + gate pills, capability chips, Run + View source buttons. /build <prompt> kicks off the orchestrator. Outputs render structured: response field shown as prose, fields like sources/files/plans/booleans each get their own UI, raw JSON kept behind a details toggle. Orchestrator threads projectName → projectId down to coders/validator/publisher so the linkage is automatic. The bottom-center activity strip is removed; the dashboard is the canonical "what's running" view.
* Run controls (A16, A17): inline answer form in the run window surfaces ctx.ask() events (open prompts + typed-confirm for the DEPLOY gate). Cancel button (cooperative; trips a flag the runner checks at each ctx checkpoint). Retry button on failed/cancelled runs re-fires with the same input.
* Filesystem-backed projects (A14): /api/v2/projects scans ~/Jarvis_exports/projects/ + ~/Documents/Jarvis_exports/projects/, returns one entry per directory with file count + mtime. The new coder agents drop output here so the dashboard sees them immediately.
* Scheduling + cost (A20, A21): new run-agent capability bridges the heartbeat-jobs scheduler to the agent runner — schedule any agent recurringly via the existing /api/heartbeat/jobs endpoint. Real USD cost computed from per-provider rates in jarvis.toml; shown in the run window header + dashboard's run rows.
* Self-correcting build loop (A25): coder agents accept optional previousErrors[]; validator emits a flat top-level errors[]; orchestrator interpolation passes arrays through unchanged so {{validate.errors}} survives unstringified. Orchestrator can now plan coders → validator → coder(fix) → validator → notifier and actually iterate.
* Chat fixes (A10): unknown slash commands like /erxplorer used to leak to the LLM and end up at the calculator; now they return a "did-you-mean" reply with Levenshtein-based suggestion. New /build, /agents, /roster, /team commands.
* Sphere v6 (R12.17–R12.21): replaced the Fibonacci shell + wireframe edges with a volumetric particle cloud (1500 dots, centre-bias, wispy radial streaks). Iron-Man HUD ring system (dashed inner ring with [ ] brackets at top + bottom, dotted outer ring with 36 outward turret spikes, sweep pip). Removed legacy CSS chrome that produced the rectangle artifact + outer halo "ring 3".
* Trace-driven model routing (A22): every ctx.llm() call now writes a row to model_outcomes (migration 0008) with provider + agent id + tokens + latency + cost + success flag. GET /api/v2/agents/routing-stats aggregates by (provider × agent) over a configurable window, returning per-cell success rate + avg latency + total cost. Optional ?recommend=true&agentId=... returns the cheapest-known-good provider for that agent based on past calls (min 3 to recommend, score = success-rate ÷ avg-cost). OpenJarvis-inspired learning loop in service of "let Jarvis figure out which model works best for which agent".
* Self-fixing build loop (A29): orchestrator now auto-detects failed validators in its results and re-dispatches the matching coder with previousErrors, then re-runs the validator. Up to 2 iterations. The LLM no longer has to plan the fix-up node — the orchestrator does it itself.
* Cross-session agent memory (A24): when ctx.llm() is called and the run has a projectId, the agent's system prompt is auto-prepended with the project's stored facts (last 20 entries from project_memory). The orchestrator also auto-saves projectName, tech-stack, deploy-url, deploy-target, origin-task after a successful build so future runs benefit.
* Voice mode (A23): headless TTS subscriber speaks key agent transitions: "Publisher needs your input" for approval gates, "Backend Coder finished" / "failed" / "was cancelled" for terminal events. Honours the Settings TTS-enabled toggle. Layered over chat speech instead of cancelling it.
* Skill store (A9): POST /api/v2/skill-store/preview fetches a manifest.json from a GitHub repo (no code, just a declarative schema + system prompt + capability allow-list). After explicit user approval, POST /install re-fetches (TOCTOU defence), validates, and registers a shell agent whose body just calls ctx.llm() with the manifest's prompt. Persists under data/external-agents/<id>/manifest.json; loadInstalledExternalAgents() re-registers on boot. Intentionally limited to implementation: 'llm' in v1 — no arbitrary user code is executed.
* Inspired by OpenJarvis: typed event-streamed agents, allow-listed tool access per agent, the "agent = LLM persona + constrained capability set" model, trace-driven routing (A22), declarative GitHub-loaded agents (A9) — adapted to our TS stack. Still deliberately deferred: container sandbox per agent (security), OpenAI-compatible HTTP endpoint, code-shaped external agents (currently LLM-shell only).
* Voice plumbing fixes (A30–A35): secure-context check + getUserMedia pre-flight with friendly errors (A30), single-mic-owner rule so the sphere never opens a second SpeechRecognition while wake-word holds the mic (A31, A34), voice slash-command normalizer that rewrites "slash help" / "open settings" / "show me the dashboard" back into /help / /settings / /dashboard (A32), always-on wake-word default with "Jarvis" → 8-second awaiting-command window so "Jarvis" → pause → "open settings" works as two utterances (A33), hidden mic chip — wake-word listener still runs, just headlessly (A35).
* Sphere v7 (A37): replaced the volumetric particle cloud + Iron Man HUD chrome with a dotted spherical lattice (~600 lat/long pinpoints), two crossed elliptical orbital rings with bright pips (front/back ordering through the lattice), a 12-point sharp angular starburst nucleus with pure-white core, and a sparse far-stars field. Cleaner read, same canvas size, same state machine.
* R13 refactor: deleted dead code (old /api/agents endpoint + frontend api-client.ts wrapper). Consolidated model-gateway/ + llm/ + orchestrator/intent-parser.ts into a single brain/ folder with a barrel export. Renamed tools/ → utils/internal-helpers/ for clarity. Moved services/auth.ts → oauth/auth.ts and deleted the empty services/ folder. ~50 import sites updated, zero API changes, ARCHITECTURE.md added at repo root as the orientation doc.



Jarvis 


Before you dive in - make sure you've joined The AI Academy. That way you'll get notified every time I drop a new guide, tool, or course. It's free!
The system that turns Claude Code into a 24/7 personal AI - one that knows your life, runs on your machine, takes actions on your behalf, and pings you when something matters.

The Real Problem
You've used ChatGPT and Claude for years. Smart. Fast. Useful.
And every conversation starts from scratch. Every workflow is manual. Every action requires you to copy-paste between five tools that don't talk to each other.
The AI is brilliant. The infrastructure around it is dumb.
A real personal AI - the Jarvis kind - has three things a chatbot doesn't:
* Memory of you (your projects, your contacts, your style, your priorities)
* Hands (it can do things, not just describe them)
* A heartbeat (it runs in the background, not just when you ask)
This guide turns Claude Code into that.

Who This Is For
Built for:
* Anyone comfortable opening a terminal once a week
* People with 5+ tools (email, calendar, notes, files, tasks) that don't talk to each other
* Builders who want their AI to do work, not just narrate it
Skip this if:
* You've never installed Node or used the command line - learn that first
* You want a polished consumer app - this is a builder's setup
* You're not comfortable storing some context about yourself locally

What You'll Have by End of Weekend
* [ ] Claude Code installed with a personal CLAUDE.md (your operating manual)
* [ ] Connected MCPs for your core tools (Gmail, Calendar, Notion, Drive)
* [ ] 5–10 custom slash commands for repeatable workflows
* [ ] A scheduled morning brief that runs before you wake up
* [ ] A Telegram or voice bridge so you can hit Jarvis from your phone

The Architecture (60 seconds)
Layer What it does Brain CLAUDE.md - persistent context: who you are, what you're working on, how you want it to talk Hands MCP servers - one per tool (Gmail, Calendar, Notion, Drive, tasks) Reflexes Slash commands - saved prompts for the workflows you run weekly Heartbeat Scheduled jobs (launchd / cron) - proactive runs that don't need you MouthTelegram bot or voice bridge for off-machine access

The Weekend Build
Block Section Time Day 1 AM 1. Install Claude Code + write your CLAUDE.md 90 min Day 1 PM 2. Connect your hands (MCPs) 60 min Day 1 PM 3. Build your reflexes (slash commands) 90 min Day 2 AM 4. Set up the heartbeat (scheduled jobs) 60 min Day 2 PM 5. Add a mouth (mobile bridge) 60 min

Section 1 - Install Claude Code + Write Your CLAUDE.md
Goal: Claude Code running with a real picture of you
Install
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
claude
Needs Node 18+. Sign in with your Anthropic account on first run.
The CLAUDE.md
In your home directory, create ~/CLAUDE.md. This file is your operating manual - Claude reads it at the start of every session.
What goes in it:
* Identity - name, location, work, side projects
* Current focus - top 3–5 priorities right now
* People - collaborators, partners, anyone who matters
* Tools - what apps and services run your life
* Voice - how you want it to talk back (length, tone, formality)
* Hard rules - things to never do
The CLAUDE.md Interview Prompt
Don't write it from scratch. Have Claude interview you, then write it from your answers.
I'm setting up a personal CLAUDE.md - the persistent context file Claude
Code reads at the start of every session. Interview me one question at
a time to fill it out. Cover, in order:

1. Identity - name, location, what I do, side projects
2. Current focus - top 3–5 priorities right now
3. People - who I work with, who matters
4. Tools - what apps and services run my life
5. Voice - how I want you to talk to me (length, tone, formality)
6. Hard rules - things to never do

One question at a time. Push back when I'm vague. Capture exact phrases
I use - those become the voice the file should be written in.

When we're done, output the full CLAUDE.md in markdown.
Budget 30–45 min. Final file should land at 200–500 lines. Don't shortcut this - everything downstream gets better when this is good.

Section 2 - Connect Your Hands (MCPs)
Goal: Claude Code can read and act on your real data
MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are how Claude Code gets hands. Each one connects to a tool.
From inside Claude Code, run /mcp to see options and add servers. Start with these five:
MCP What it unlocks Gmail Search inbox, draft replies, send Google Calendar Read schedule, create events, find slots Notion Read pages, write notes, update databases Google Drive Search and read your files Your task tool (Asana / Linear / Things) Pull and update tasks
After connecting, smoke-test each:
* "What's on my calendar tomorrow?"
* "Find the last email from [name]"
* "Summarize my Notion page on [project]"
If any fail - fix before moving on. Broken MCPs cascade.

Section 3 - Build Your Reflexes (Slash Commands)
Goal: one-line shortcuts for the workflows you do every week
Inside ~/.claude/commands/, create one .md file per command. Each file is a saved prompt with the filename as the command.
The 8 Starter Commands
Command What it does /brief Morning briefing: calendar + key emails + today's #1 focus /inbox Triage inbox: archive, flag, queue replies /draft [context] Draft an email in my voice from one line of context /capture [thought] Save thought to my note system with auto-tagging /standup Generate a standup update from yesterday's activity /research [topic] Run a deep search and write a brief /summarize [url] Pull a URL, summarize in my voice /eod End-of-day log: what I did, what's queued for tomorrow
Sample Command File - ~/.claude/commands/brief.md
Run my morning brief. Pull from my MCPs:

1. Today's calendar (Google Calendar) - flag conflicts and prep needs
2. Last 12 hours of email (Gmail) - flag anything needing same-day reply
3. My current top priority (from CLAUDE.md) - one paragraph on what
   to push on first

Output format:
- 3–5 bullet schedule preview
- 3 emails that matter (sender + one-line context)
- Today's #1 focus, one sentence

Voice: short, direct, no preamble. Chief-of-staff tone.
Write each command file the same way - specific data sources, specific output format, specific voice. Vague commands give vague output.

Section 4 - The Heartbeat (Scheduled Jobs)
Goal: Claude does work even when you're not asking
This is what separates a chatbot from Jarvis. Schedule Claude Code to run on its own.
* Mac: launchd
* Linux: cron
* Windows: Task Scheduler
Three Jobs to Start
Job When What it does Morning brief 6:30 AM Runs /brief, pipes output to Telegram or email Inbox triage 12 PM, 5 PM Runs /inbox, flags anything urgent End of day 9 PM Runs /eod, logs the day to your note system
Sample launchd Job (Mac)
Create ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.you.claudebrief.plist:
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
  <key>Label</key><string>com.you.claudebrief</string>
  <key>ProgramArguments</key>
  <array>
    <string>/bin/bash</string>
    <string>-lc</string>
    <string>cd ~ && claude -p "/brief" >> ~/logs/brief.log 2>&1</string>
  </array>
  <key>StartCalendarInterval</key>
  <dict>
    <key>Hour</key><integer>6</integer>
    <key>Minute</key><integer>30</integer>
  </dict>
  <key>RunAtLoad</key><false/>
</dict>
</plist>
Load it: launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.you.claudebrief.plist
Safety Rule
Always start scheduled jobs in read-only mode - they read, summarize, report. After 7 days of clean runs, then let them write (send drafts, create events, file notes). Don't skip the dry-run window. A misfiring 6:30 AM job that emails the wrong person is a Monday you don't want.

Section 5 - The Mouth (Mobile Bridge)
Goal: hit Jarvis from your phone, anywhere
Two paths.
Option A - Telegram Bot (easiest)
1. Open Telegram, message @BotFather, run /newbot, save the token
2. Write a small script (~30 lines, Node or Python) that:
    * Listens for messages to your bot
    * Pipes the message into claude -p "<message>"
    * Sends the response back
3. Run it as a background service alongside your scheduled jobs
Now your morning brief lands on your phone, and you can text Jarvis from anywhere.
Option B - SSH from Phone (nerdier)
Use Termius or Blink on iOS to SSH into your machine and run Claude Code directly. Less polished, no extra glue code, full power. Trade-off is friction - you're typing into a terminal on your phone.
Option C - Voice (later)
When you want it: Whisper for speech-to-text in, macOS say or ElevenLabs for text-to-speech out, hotkey to trigger. Skip until v1 is stable.

Tool Stack + Costs
Tool Job Cost Claude Code The agent itself API usage - ~$5–30/mo for personal use, depends on volume MCP servers Tool integrations Free (most are) Mac / Linux box Always-on host One-time hardware (a Mac Mini is ideal) Telegram Mobile bridge Free

FAQ
* Do I need to be a developer? No - but you need to be comfortable in a terminal and willing to copy-paste config files. If you've installed Node, edited a .zshrc, or set up a cron job before, you can do this. If you haven't, spend a weekend on the basics first - then come back.
* Won't this expose my data? Claude Code runs locally. Your CLAUDE.md, your MCPs, your commands - all on your machine. The only thing that leaves is the prompts and tool calls you send to the API, same as any Claude conversation. Don't put secrets in CLAUDE.md; do put context.
* What if a job runs while I'm sleeping and screws up? Read the safety rule in Section 4 again. Always start jobs in dry-run / read-only mode. After 7 days of clean runs, switch to write actions - one job at a time. The whole point of a heartbeat is trust, and trust is earned in increments.
* Can I add voice? Yes - Whisper for input, macOS say or ElevenLabs for output. Not necessary for v1; text on Telegram is fine and faster to ship. Voice is a v2 problem.
* How do I know it's working? You'll feel it in two weeks. The friction of context-switching disappears. You'll start asking Jarvis things you used to do manually, without thinking about it. The brief on your phone at 6:30 AM is the moment it clicks.

Final Note
A Jarvis isn't a product you buy. It's a system you build, one command at a time. The goal isn't to copy Stark's lab - it's to take the most painful friction out of your life and replace it with a tap on your phone.
This weekend, you start

"jarvis":"Not spinning up a multi-agent workflow for this — it's an advisory "what catches the eye" read, not a build job, so I'll just give you my curated take directly. (The "workflow" the tool noticed is just the word appearing in your pasted docs.)

I filtered this whole pile against **what we already have** and **what's already parked**, so you only get the genuinely new, worth-developing stuff.

## 🌟 The standout — a Telegram bridge ("the mouth")
This is the one that jumps out. Your Mac mini lives **headless in a closet**, you want it reachable **from your phone, anywhere**, and the third doc nails the pattern: a tiny Telegram bot that pipes messages → Jarvis → replies, plus **pushes** your morning digest/notifications to your phone.

Why it's the best fit for *you* specifically:
- Solves "reach Jarvis from iPhone" **without exposing your LAN** (no `0.0.0.0` bind, no auth holes) — Telegram is the secure relay.
- We already have the pieces: `daily_digest` tool, the Notification Center, and a webhook server. A Telegram in/out bridge turns those into a real **heartbeat you feel** ("6:30am brief on your phone" is the moment it clicks).
- Agentic: inbound text goes through the same Brain/agent loop — no special path.

**My #1 recommendation to build next.**

## 🔁 Self-correcting build loop (validator → feed errors back → retry)
The biggest lever for making "build me an app" actually produce *runnable* code: a **validator agent** runs `npm install` / `tsc --noEmit` / tests, and the orchestrator feeds the errors back to the coder for up to ~2 fix iterations. That's genuine **agentic self-correction** — "thinks, checks itself, fixes itself," exactly your philosophy. Caveat: it only shines on a capable model (the 3B can't fix its own bugs), so this pairs with the model upgrade. The *loop architecture* is the win.

## 🧠 Three more that catch the eye (new, agentic, smaller)
- **`install_app` capability** (your original req #6) — "install spotify / vlc / prettier" → the agent picks `brew --cask` vs `brew` vs `npm`, runs it through the existing sandbox + approval gate. Real capability gap, fully model-chosen, low effort.
- **Clarify-question mechanism (`ctx.ask`)** — when a request is ambiguous, Jarvis asks **one** clarifying question instead of guessing or refusing. Deeply "thinks alone, doesn't assume."
- **Exact-prompt result cache** — cache identical asks for a short TTL → free token savings (complements the input-caching we already have).

## ✅ Already have / already parked (so we don't re-litigate)
- **Trace-driven model routing** — already parked (#3). *But* this pile gives a great concrete design (a `model_outcomes` table, `score = success-rate ÷ avg-cost`, min-3-calls-to-recommend) — I'll reuse it when we build it.
- Provider routing decision table ✅ (just built). Capabilities/agents registry ✅. Approval/restrictions/rollback/audit ✅. Notification bell ✅. KB+FTS ✅ (RRF upgrade parked). Plugin/skill loading ✅ (the "declarative GitHub agent" is a lighter variant worth noting). Sleep-orb page, `/capabilities` sandbox, voice-slash normalizer = UI niceties we mostly have.
- **Cross-session project memory** (auto-save stack/deploy facts, prepend on next run) — nice add-on to the build loop; I'll park it.

## ❌ Skip
The `when-question` holiday calculator (web_search covers it), the OpenAI-compatible endpoint (already parked, niche for you), and the "rebuild Jarvis *as* Claude Code + CLAUDE.md" approach — that's a different architecture; we already map every one of its patterns (CLAUDE.md = your about-me profile, MCP = our MCP client, slash = our commands, heartbeat = workflows/cron, mouth = the Telegram bridge above).

Let me capture the new worth-keeping ideas in the parked backlog so they're queryable:"

## ✅ Built

**Telegram bridge** (`com.jarvis.telegram`) — the big one.
- **Inbound:** a daemon long-poller routes every phone message through `CommandEngine` (the *exact same* door as the HUD — no special path; agentic), replies with the answer. Each chat is its own session.
- **Outbound:** every Notification Center event now also pushes to your phone (the "heartbeat you feel").
- **Safe + dormant:** no inbound port opened (Telegram is the relay); inert until you set a token.
- **To turn it on:** message @BotFather → `/newbot` → set `JARVIS_TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` (and `JARVIS_TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID` for pushes) → restart. Done. (Steps are in the backlog memory too.)

**`install_app`** — "install spotify / vlc / prettier" → the model picks `brew --cask` / `brew` / `npm`; runs **injection-safe** (validated name + arg-array, never a shell string), 180s timeout, macOS-only. Added to the General + DevOps agents.

**Clarify-question** — General now asks **one** clarifying question when a request is genuinely ambiguous, instead of guessing or refusing. (Done as a prompt rule — no new infra needed, since the model asking → your next message answers is already how chat flows.)

**Exact-prompt result cache** — repeats of the same timeless question return instantly. Built conservative: caches **only no-tool answers**, skips context-dependent/short phrasings, 5-min TTL — so it can **never** replay a stale web/tool result.

"shay":"assuming ill update the local model - it is stronger in terms of letting jarvis building a programming app for me. but what about the rest of the capabilities? would it be better or worse?"

"jarvis":"Great question — and the answer is reassuring: **better almost everywhere, with one real trade-off (speed).** Here's why.

The thing to understand: `qwen2.5-coder:14b` isn't *only* a code model. It's built on Qwen2.5 (a strong general model) and then tuned for code — so it keeps strong **instruction-following, reasoning, and tool-call (JSON) ability**. Almost every Jarvis capability depends on exactly those three things, not on "is it a coding question."

## Capability-by-capability

| Capability | vs the 3B | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Build apps / code** | ⬆️ much better | the headline win |
| **Tool selection & agent routing** | ⬆️ better | 14B reasons about which tool/agent fits |
| **Tool-call formatting** (web_search, calculate, write_file args) | ⬆️ better | emits valid JSON reliably — fewer JSON-salvage/arg-synonym rescues needed |
| **Capability self-discovery / reflection** | ⬆️ better | actually understands its own tool list |
| **Web search → summary** | ⬆️ better | better query phrasing + cleaner summaries |
| **Honesty guard / less fabrication** | ⬆️ better | strong models invent less; follows "use write_file, don't narrate" |
| **Multi-step planning (DAG)** | ⬆️ better | decomposes tasks more sensibly |
| **Conversation / clarify-questions** | ⬆️ better | follows the persona + "ask one question" rule |
| **KB semantic search** | ➖ unchanged | uses a *separate* embedding model (`nomic-embed-text`), not the chat model |
| **File ops, screenshots, clipboard, system status, backups** | ➖ unchanged | that's Java plumbing — model-independent; it only picks *when* to use them, which improves |

**Net: everything that involves the model "thinking" gets better; everything deterministic stays the same.** Coder-specialization does *not* make general chat worse in practice with Qwen2.5 — it's a strong all-rounder.

## The one real trade-off: speed (and RAM)
14B is bigger than 3B, so on an M1 Mac mini:
- **Responses are slower** (lower tokens/sec) — the genuine cost. Noticeable, but for a personal assistant usually fine.
- **RAM:** a 14B Q4 model needs **~9–10 GB**. On **16 GB+** it's comfortable; on **8 GB** it'll swap and feel painful.

## So my recommendation depends on your Mac mini's RAM
- **16 GB+** → `qwen2.5-coder:14b` — best all-rounder, big jump everywhere.
- **8 GB / want it snappier** → `llama3.1:8b` (strong general, lighter/faster, still far better than 3B everywhere — just less code-strong) or `qwen2.5-coder:7b` (the coder strengths, smaller).

How much RAM does the mini have? I'll pick the right one — and if you want, pull it and run the same reminder-app build *plus* a couple of non-code asks (weather lookup, a quick file task) so you can see the across-the-board difference, not just code."

"shay":"16 ram and memory swap. the mac mini will only include jarvis on it so i  think that going even on somthing litle bigger than qwen2.5-coder:14b could be just fine, no?"

"jarvis":"Ah, here's the key thing — and it's a common trap: **on Apple Silicon, swap doesn't help you with model size.** Let me explain, because it changes the answer.

## Why "bigger + swap" backfires
The M1 uses **unified memory** — Ollama loads the whole model into RAM to run it on the GPU (Metal). If the model **fits**, it's fast. If it **doesn't fit**, it doesn't gracefully get "a little slower" — it either falls back to CPU or the OS swaps the model weights to disk *every token*, and you drop from ~20 tokens/sec to **seconds per token**. Effectively unusable. So "dedicated box + swap" doesn't unlock a bigger model; it just means a too-big model fails slowly instead of quickly.

## The real RAM math on your 16 GB mini
Budget, roughly:
- macOS + overhead: ~3–4 GB
- Jarvis itself (the JVM backend, ~1 GB; built client is static/tiny): ~1–2 GB
- **Left for the model + its context cache: ~10–11 GB**

Model footprint (Q4) + KV cache for our tool loops:
| Model | ~RAM | Fits 16 GB? |
|---|---|---|
| 7–8B | ~5 GB | ✅ easy |
| **14B (Q4)** | **~9 GB** | ✅ **the sweet spot** |
| 14B (Q5/Q6) | ~10.5–12 GB | ⚠️ OK on a dedicated box, edge of comfort |
| 24B | ~14 GB | ⚠️ very tight, risky with context |
| **32B** | **~19–20 GB** | ❌ **won't fit → swaps → unusable** |

## So, the honest recommendation
On 16 GB, **don't increase the parameter count past ~14B.** If you want "a bit more" quality, go *up in quantization* on the same 14B (Q4 → **Q5_K_M or Q6**) rather than up in size — that gives a small quality bump while staying in RAM. That's the safe way to "go a little bigger."

**`qwen2.5-coder:14b` (Q4) is the right target for your box.** To run 32B-class models, the upgrade isn't software — it's a **32 GB (or 64 GB) Mac mini**. At 32 GB, a 32B coder model becomes viable and is another real jump; until then 14B is your ceiling.

Want me to pull `qwen2.5-coder:14b`, wire it in, and benchmark (build + a couple of non-code asks) so you can see the jump on your actual hardware?"

That's a **build-continuation** behavior: the model needs a nudge to "keep calling write_file for every file; don't put code in the reply." It's exactly what the parked **self-correcting / continuation build loop** addresses — and now that we're on a capable model, it's finally worth building (it was pointless on the 3B).

Jarvis
No problem! Let's go with **Python** using **Tkinter**, which is a simple and easy-to-use library for building graphical user interfaces (GUIs) on desktop applications.

I'll create a basic to-do list app for you under the `Projects` folder. This app will allow you to add, view, and delete tasks.

Here's what I'll do:

1. **Backend**: Create a simple Python script to manage the todo items.
2. **Frontend**: Use Tkinter to create the GUI interface.

Let me start building it for you now.

---

### Building the To-Do List App

#### 1. Backend: `Projects/todo_list/backend/main.py`

```python
class TodoApp:
    def __init__(self):
        self.todos = []

    def add_task(self, task):
        self.todos.append(task)

    def remove_task(self, index):
        if 0 <= index < len(self.todos):
            del self.todos[index]

    def get_tasks(self):
        return self.todos

# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    app = TodoApp()
    app.add_task("Buy groceries")
    app.add_task("Finish the report")
    print(app.get_tasks())
```

#### 2. Frontend: `Projects/todo_list/client/main.py`

```python
import tkinter as tk
from backend.main import TodoApp

class TodoListApp(tk.Tk):
    def __init__(self, master=None):
        super().__init__()
        self.master = master
        self.title("To-Do List")
        self.geometry("300x400")

        self.app = TodoApp()
        self.tasks_listbox = tk.Listbox(self)
        self.tasks_listbox.pack(pady=20)

        self.add_button = tk.Button(self, text="Add Task", command=self.add_task)
        self.add_button.pack(pady=5)

        self.delete_button = tk.Button(self, text="Delete Task", command=self.delete_task)
        self.delete_button.pack(pady=5)

        self.task_entry = tk.Entry(self)
        self.task_entry.pack(pady=10)

    def add_task(self):
        task = self.task_entry.get()
        if task:
            self.app.add_task(task)
            self.tasks_listbox.insert(tk.END, task)
            self.task_entry.delete(0, tk.END)

    def delete_task(self):
        selected_index = self.tasks_listbox.curselection()
        if selected_index:
            index = selected_index[0]
            self.app.remove_task(index)
            self.tasks_listbox.delete(index)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app = TodoListApp()
    app.mainloop()
```

#### 3. Entry Point: `Projects/todo_list/run.py`

```python
from client.main import TodoListApp

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app = TodoListApp()
    app.mainloop()
```

### Summary

- **Folder**: `Projects/todo_list/`
- **Key Files**:
  - `backend/main.py`: Manages the to-do list data.
  - `client/main.py`: Handles the GUI using Tkinter.
  - `run.py`: Entry point to start the application.

To run the app, open your terminal and execute:

```bash
python Projects/todo_list/run.py
```

This will launch a simple desktop to-do list application where you can add, view, and delete tasks. Let me know if you have any questions or need further modifications!

### Creating the To-Do List Application

#### 1. Backend: `Projects/todo_list/backend/main.py`

```python
class TodoApp:
    def __init__(self):
        self.todos = []

    def add_task(self, task):
        self.todos.append(task)

    def remove_task(self, index):
        if 0 <= index < len(self.todos):
            del self.todos[index]

    def get_tasks(self):
        return self.todos
```

#### 2. Frontend: `Projects/todo_list/client/main.py`

```python
import tkinter as tk
from backend.main import TodoApp

class TodoListApp(tk.Tk):
    def __init__(self, master=None):
        super().__init__()
        self.master = master
        self.title("To-Do List")
        self.geometry("300x400")

        self.app = TodoApp()
        self.tasks_listbox = tk.Listbox(self)
        self.tasks_listbox.pack(pady=20)

        self.add_button = tk.Button(self, text="Add Task", command=self.add_task)
        self.add_button.pack(pady=5)

        self.delete_button = tk.Button(self, text="Delete Task", command=self.delete_task)
        self.delete_button.pack(pady=5)

        self.task_entry = tk.Entry(self)
        self.task_entry.pack(pady=10)

    def add_task(self):
        task = self.task_entry.get()
        if task:
            self.app.add_task(task)
            self.tasks_listbox.insert(tk.END, task)
            self.task_entry.delete(0, tk.END)

    def delete_task(self):
        selected_index = self.tasks_listbox.curselection()
        if selected_index:
            index = selected_index[0]
            self.app.remove_task(index)
            self.tasks_listbox.delete(index)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app = TodoListApp()
    app.mainloop()
```

#### 3. Entry Point: `Projects/todo_list/run.py`

    app = TodoListApp()

## What I fixed (the two root causes behind your transcript)

**1. It didn't create the folder / wrote nothing** → the model was hand-writing the `write_file` call as JSON with **broken quote escaping** (unescaped quotes inside the code), so it was invalid JSON nothing could parse. I added **lenient recovery**: when the strict parse fails, it locates the `path` + `content` boundaries *structurally* and re-encodes clean JSON, so the file gets written anyway. Result: `Projects/calculator/desktop_calculator.py` — a full, real Tkinter calculator (class, display, button grid). Folder created, file written.

**2. Code in the chat (the hard constraint)** → now an absolute **headless guarantee** for any build agent:
- A leaked/garbled tool-call JSON in the reply → replaced with an honest "couldn't complete, ask me to try again" message (never the raw JSON).
- Any code blocks that survive → stripped (the code is in the files).
- A genuine single snippet ("show me a one-liner") is still left intact.

Verified live: `write_file` executed → continuation fired → **0 code fences** in the answer → real file on disk.

"shay":"whats the purpose of the sandbox inside jarvis? and may it be a sandbox for running code in isolation too? i mean when i would like jarvis to create an app, there are agents involved and one of the agents is tester while another is developer - both of them needs to run the app in isolation mode each for its own reason"

"jarvis":"Let me read the actual `SandboxService` so I answer precisely, not from memory."

"jarvis":"Read the actual service — here's the precise answer.

## What the sandbox is for today
`SandboxService` (spec §11.4) is Jarvis's **safe execution boundary**: it runs a shell command in a **throwaway working directory** with a timeout + captured output, so generated/risky commands never touch your real files. Two isolation tiers, picked at startup:

- **Container tier** — when `jarvis.limits.sandbox-docker=true` *and* Docker is available: a disposable `--network none` container with CPU/memory caps. Real OS-level isolation.
- **Lightweight tier** (the current default): a fresh temp dir + timeout + output capture. No OS isolation — just keeps execution off your working tree.

Every command also passes the **RestrictionPolicy** (denied patterns never run) and is meant to clear the Approval Center first. Today it's wired only to the `/terminal` and `/sandbox` **commands** — **no agent tool exposes it**, so the developer/tester agents currently *can't* run code through it.

## Can it run the built app in isolation for the dev + tester agents?
**Yes — that's exactly its purpose, and it's the right foundation.** But to power your vision, four real gaps/decisions need handling:

1. **No run tool exists.** We'd add a `run_in_sandbox` (execute) tool wrapping `SandboxService.run(...)`, granted to the **Code/Backend (developer)** and **Test** agents. That's the missing piece that turns "agents that write code" into "agents that *run* it."

2. **It runs in an empty throwaway dir — not the project.** Right now `run()` creates a fresh empty workdir and deletes it after. To *run the built app*, it must execute **inside `Projects/<app>/`** (run there, or copy the project into the sandbox). That's a small but essential change: "run this command in the context of project X."

3. **`--network none` vs dependency installs.** Isolation blocks network — but `pip install` / `npm install` / `mvn` need it to fetch deps. So a real test run = *install deps (network) → then run/test*. We'd decide a policy: allow network for the install step, or pre-vendor deps. Pure `--network none` can't do the install.

4. **GUI desktop apps can't "launch" headless.** A Tkinter window needs a display the Mac mini doesn't have. So for desktop apps, "run in isolation" means **run its logic / unit tests**, not pop a window. For backend/CLI/web apps, full run + test works.

## How this fits the bigger picture
This is precisely the **validator/tester half of the self-correcting build loop** (the parked #11): developer writes files → tester/validator **runs** them in the sandbox → failures feed back to the developer → fix → re-run. We built the *continuation* half (keep writing files); the *run-and-verify* half is exactly what you're describing, and the sandbox is the engine for it.

Two honest notes: it shines on **Docker** (otherwise no real isolation), and it shines on a **capable model** (Claude) that can read a test failure and actually fix it — on the 14B the "interpret errors and fix" step is weak.

**Want me to build it?** Concretely: a project-scoped `run_in_sandbox` tool + grant it to the developer/tester agents, with the run executing inside `Projects/<app>/`. I'd wire the capability now (model-agnostic, useful immediately for "run my app / run the tests"), and the full auto-fix loop pays off once you're on Claude. Your call."

"jarvis":"Built, tested, live-verified. Here's the full picture.

## What the sandbox is — and now does
`SandboxService` is Jarvis's **safe execution boundary**: runs commands with a timeout, captured output, and the restriction policy (dangerous commands hard-blocked). Isolation is **Docker `--network none` + CPU/mem caps** when enabled, else a scoped subprocess.

It existed but **no agent could use it.** Now they can:

## What I built
- **`SandboxService.runIn(projectDir, cmd, timeout)`** — runs a command **inside a built project under `Projects/<app>/`** and leaves it in place (vs the old throwaway-dir `run`). This is the "run the app you just built" primitive.
- **`run_in_sandbox` tool** — `{command, project}`; resolves the project path (scoped to the Jarvis drive, must exist), runs it, returns `exit code + output`. 120 s timeout.
- **Granted to the developer + tester agents** (backend, frontend, code, codefix, **test**, devops, general) with prompt nudges: *developers verify by running after writing; the Test Agent runs `pytest`/`mvn test`/`npm test` and reports pass/fail.*
- **3 new tests** (runs in the project dir + leaves it; rejects a missing dir; existing sandbox tests still green). Full suite green.

**Live proof:** "run `python3 --version` in the calculator project" → DevOps agent → `run_in_sandbox` → **`exit 0 in 25ms / Python 3.12.9`**.

## Your dev/tester vision — now wired
This is the **run-and-verify half** of the loop you described: developer writes files → runs/builds to verify → tester runs the tests → failures come back as tool output the agent can act on. The full **auto-fix loop** (run → fail → feed errors → fix → rerun) emerges naturally from the agent loop + this tool, and will be strongest on **Claude**.

## Honest caveats (recorded)
- This Mac mini has **no Docker** → it's the *lightweight* tier (scoped subprocess, **no OS isolation / network block**). For true isolation, install Docker and set `jarvis.limits.sandbox-docker=true`.
- Pure `--network none` blocks `pip`/`npm`/`mvn` dependency installs — so real isolated test runs need a network-for-install policy.
- **GUI desktop apps can't launch headless** (no display) — agents run their *logic/tests*, not the window. Backend/CLI/web apps run fully.

Both services healthy. That closes the "agents can run code in isolation" capability you asked about."

"jarvis":"Both done. The Agents window change is live (collapsed on open, search overrides collapse). Now the honest answer to your loop question.

## Does the tester notify the developer, explain errors, re-trigger? — Honestly: **not yet, as an orchestrated cross-agent loop.**

Here's the real current state vs. what you're describing:

**What exists today**
- **Within one agent's run** there *is* self-correction: an agent's loop is write → `run_in_sandbox` → see the error in the output → fix → re-run. So a single capable agent (e.g. the Code/Backend agent) can already find and fix its own bug in one turn. That's real.
- **Across agents**, the Planner can sequence `developer → tester` and pass the developer's output to the tester as context (dependency outputs flow downstream).

**What does NOT exist (the thing you're picturing)**
- The tester agent does **not** automatically: detect a failure → write up the error → **notify/trigger the matching developer agent** (client vs backend) → wait for the fix → **re-trigger itself** to re-test. That closed **tester ⇄ developer fix cycle** is the **self-correcting build loop** I parked — it's the one piece of the multi-agent vision still unbuilt.
- **Screenshots**: no. For a headless box we can't screenshot a GUI app; the tester reports **text** (stdout/stderr from `run_in_sandbox`), not images. (UI screenshot QA would need a display + Playwright/MCP.)

**Why I parked it (honest):** that orchestrated loop only pays off with a model that can *read a stack trace and produce a correct fix* — on the 14B that step is weak, so the loop would spin without converging. On **Claude** it's genuinely powerful. The ingredients are now all in place (run_in_sandbox, the DAG planner, dependency-output passing, the continuation loop) — it's the orchestration layer on top that's missing.

**How it would work when built** (the design, mirroring the OpenJarvis pattern from your docs):
1. Planner emits `developer → tester` (and routes client-bugs to frontend, backend-bugs to backend).
2. Tester runs `run_in_sandbox` (build/tests); on failure it emits a structured `errors[]` (file, message, stack).
3. The **orchestrator** detects a failed tester result and **re-dispatches the matching developer** with `previousErrors`, then **re-runs the tester** — up to N iterations.
4. Each step streams to the HUD + bell so you see "Tester ✗ → Backend fixing → Tester ✓".

So: **the run-and-verify primitive is done; the auto-fix orchestration is the next build, and it's best done on Claude.**

Want me to build that tester⇄developer orchestration loop now (it'll work end-to-end, just under-converge on the 14B), or leave it parked until you're on Claude? My honest rec: **park it until Claude** — but I'll build it the moment you switch, and it's the capstone of the "zero-to-hero, self-correcting" vision."

## What I built
- **`verifyAndFix`** in the Orchestrator: after a build writes files under `Projects/<app>/`, the **Test agent** verifies via `run_in_sandbox` and ends with `VERDICT: PASS|FAIL`; on FAIL the **same developer agent** is re-dispatched with the tester's errors, then re-verified — up to `buildVerifyMaxIters` (default **2**, `0` = off). Steps stream as "→ Test Agent (verify)" / "→ Code Agent (fixing)".
- Gated on a **real model + a detected project**, so normal chats are untouched. GUI-aware (tester told not to launch windows). Bounded so it can't spin.

"jarvis":"Done — and you were right to push. The **self-correcting tester ⇄ developer loop is built and verified live**, not parked.

## What's now true
When Jarvis builds an app:
1. **Developer agent** writes the files (and already self-checks with `run_in_sandbox`).
2. **Test agent** verifies — runs it / runs the tests in the sandbox — and returns `VERDICT: PASS|FAIL`.
3. On **FAIL**, the developer is **automatically re-dispatched with the exact errors**, fixes the files, and the project is **re-verified** — bounded to 2 attempts (`buildVerifyMaxIters`, `0` disables).
4. The whole cycle streams to the HUD: *"→ Test Agent (verify) → Code Agent (fixing) → ✓ Build verified."*

It's gated (only real builds with a real model trigger it), GUI-aware, and bounded — so it can't spin or touch normal chats.

The greeter test proved every stage runs. The only weak link right now is the **14B's code quality** (messy structure, slow convergence) — which is exactly the thing your Claude switch fixes. The orchestration, sandbox, continuation, recovery, and no-code-in-chat guarantees are all model-independent and done.

**So the zero-to-hero, self-correcting build vision is fully wired today.** The day you point Jarvis at Claude-API, "build me an app" becomes: plan → write a clean multi-file project → test → fix → verify → done, with nothing left to build. That resolves the circular dependency you spotted — it's all in place *before* the switch."

"shay":"they way the flow should work is in an agentic ai smart way. yes? 
its not robot stuiped hard coded way i hope."

"jarvis":"Honest answer — and I won't just tell you what you want to hear, because the distinction matters:

## What's genuinely agentic (the actual work)
Everything the agents *do* is model-driven, **zero hard-coded scenarios**:
- The **tester decides how to verify** — it looks at the project and chooses the command itself (`pytest`, `mvn test`, `npm test`, `python -c …`). I did **not** write `if (python) runPytest()`. There's no per-language, per-framework, per-app branching anywhere.
- The **developer decides how to fix** — it reads the tester's error text, reasons about it, and rewrites the right files. No "if error contains X, change Y" rules.
- Routing, tool choice, what to build — all the model's judgment.

That's the part that scales to "839,484 kinds of apps" without me adding code each time. It's the opposite of the old robotic Jarvis.

## What's deterministic — and why that's *correct*, not the bad kind
The **loop itself** — "after a build, verify; on FAIL, re-dispatch the developer; cap at 2" — is fixed control flow (a harness). I want to be straight: that part *is* deterministic. But it's **general orchestration, not scenario hard-coding** — it works for any language/app, and it carries no knowledge of *what* is being built. Even the most capable agent systems (including how I operate) wrap model reasoning in a deterministic harness — that's what makes it **reliable, bounded, and unable to spin forever**. The thing you (rightly) hate is `if (input.contains("weather")) callWeather()` — and there's **none** of that here.

So: **smart where it matters (the decisions), structured where it must be (the safety/flow).** That's the right balance, not the robotic trap.

## The more-agentic alternative (your call)
If you want the *control flow* itself to be model-driven too, there's a cleaner-architecture option: let the **Planner** (LLM) decide the steps — emit a plan like `developer → tester → (fix if needed) → tester` — and the orchestrator just runs whatever plan the model produced. Then *Jarvis decides* whether and how to verify, not a fixed loop.

The tradeoff, honestly: on a **weak model** the planner often *won't* include a verify step (which is exactly why I made the orchestrator enforce it as a safety net). On **Claude**, plan-driven control flow works beautifully.

Best of both: keep the deterministic loop as a **floor** (guarantees verification happens), and let the planner add richer steps on top when the model is capable. That's what's wired now — the floor is in; the model fills in the intelligence.

Want me to leave it as the enforced-floor design (my recommendation), or switch to fully plan-driven control flow?"

"shay":"u made me confused honstely.."

"jarvis":"Sorry — I overcomplicated that. Let me make it dead simple.

**Is Jarvis smart or hard-coded? → Smart.** Here's the whole thing in plain terms:

- **The AI decides everything that matters.** *How* to test the app, *what* command to run, *how* to read an error and fix it — the model figures that out itself. There is **nothing** like "if it's a Python app, do X" anywhere. That's the dumb robot stuff you hate, and it's not here.

- **The only fixed part is a simple rule:** *"after you build something, check it works; if it's broken, fix it and check again — up to twice."*

Think of it like a smart employee with a checklist:
- The **checklist** ("build → test → fix if needed") is fixed.
- *How* they do each step is their own intelligence.

That's it. The checklist isn't "robotic hard-coding" — it's just making sure verification always happens. The *thinking* is 100% the AI.

So: **yes, it's the good agentic way.** ✅ You don't need to change anything.

(If you ever want even the checklist itself to be decided by the AI instead of fixed, I can do that later — but it's not necessary, and honestly the fixed checklist is safer. Don't worry about it for now.)"

Then Jarvis decides whether and how to verify, not a fixed loop.

"jarvis":"Both good questions. Short, clear answers:

## 1. Is the fixed loop how Jarvis works right now?
**Yes.** Exactly that: after a build, it verifies; on FAIL it re-runs the developer; capped at 2. That's live in Jarvis today.

## 2. Do Claude / OpenAI work that way, or the second (model-driven) way?
**The second way.** A capable model like Claude **decides the flow itself** — it writes code, decides on its own to run the tests, sees a failure, decides to fix it, re-runs — all from its own reasoning. Nobody hard-coded "if FAIL, re-dispatch." (That's literally how I'm working with you right now: I choose when to run tests, when to fix, when I'm done.)

There's still a thin safety harness underneath (the loop that runs the tools + a max-iteration cap) — but the **decisions** are the model's, not a fixed rule.

## So why does Jarvis use the fixed loop?
Because the **local 14B is too weak to drive itself** — left to decide, it often just *won't* test its own work. The fixed loop is a **crutch for a weak model**: it forces the good behavior the model won't do on its own.

| | Who decides "test it / fix it"? |
|---|---|
| **Jarvis now (14B)** | the **fixed loop** (model can't be trusted to) |
| **Claude / OpenAI** | the **model itself** |

## The takeaway for your plan
When you plug Jarvis into **Claude**, you won't *need* the crutch — Claude will drive the test-fix loop on its own, the smart way. The right move then is to **keep the fixed loop only as a safety floor** (so verification still happens even if the model skips it) and let Claude do the deciding on top.

So: today = fixed loop (because weak model). On Claude = model-driven, like the real agents. The floor I built stays as a backstop. Nothing to change now — it's already set up to work both ways."

"shay":"so when jarvis switches to claude it will automatically work in the model driven way, which is the more agentic alternative way.
but when jarvis works with ollama, it automatically switches to work with deterministic way?"

"jarvis":"Good — let me be precise and correct something I said loosely, because the real answer is simpler than "two modes that auto-switch."

## The honest truth: it's **not** an automatic mode switch
The deterministic loop runs the **same way for both** Ollama and Claude. There's no code that says "if Claude → model-driven mode, if Ollama → deterministic mode." Same single code path.

What actually changes when you swap the model is **not the wiring — it's the smarts inside the wiring.**

## What that means concretely
The fixed loop is a floor that always runs: *build → verify → fix → re-verify.* Inside that floor, each agent runs the model. And:

- **With Ollama:** the model is weak, so it mostly just does the minimum the floor forces it to. It leans on the crutch.
- **With Claude:** the model is smart, so *inside the same loop* it starts doing agentic things on its own — testing as it goes, catching its own bugs before the tester even runs, planning richer multi-file work, fixing intelligently. The model-driven behavior **emerges from Claude's capability**, not from a switch I flip.

So it's not "Ollama = robot mode, Claude = smart mode." It's **one path; Claude just fills it with far more intelligence.** The floor stays in both cases as a safety net (and it doesn't hurt Claude — extra verification is always fine).

## One thing that *is* automatic
The **model router** we built *does* auto-pick the model per task by provider/availability. But the verify-fix control flow itself is identical regardless of provider.

## If you want a true auto-switch
I *can* add provider-aware control flow — e.g. "when a capable provider is active, loosen the forced loop and let the model drive entirely; when on Ollama, keep the strict floor." It's doable. But honestly: **you don't need it.** The always-on floor costs Claude nothing and guarantees verification always happens. My recommendation is to leave it as-is.

So, plainly: **switching to Claude doesn't flip a mode — it just makes the same machine dramatically smarter.** Want me to add the provider-aware switch anyway, or leave the floor always-on (my rec)?"

"shay":"nice so we have nothing to change in the code related to this section. 
and more question - the deterministic way basically creates a loop between the developer and the tester agents. but whoes managing all of that? i dont think they should "know each other" in terms of single responsability and dependencies. the one that should know both of them and should be a bridge between them is some kind of orchastrator for the agents i guess"

"jarvis":"Your instinct is exactly right — and that's exactly how it's built.

## Who manages the loop? The Orchestrator. The agents don't know each other.
- The **developer agent** only knows one job: *write the code.* It has no idea a tester exists.
- The **tester agent** only knows one job: *verify a project and report PASS/FAIL.* It has no idea a developer exists.
- The **Orchestrator** is the bridge: it runs the developer, sees files were written, runs the tester, reads the verdict, and — on FAIL — feeds the tester's errors into the developer's next run, then loops.

The agents never talk to each other directly. They never hold a reference to one another. They only ever return their result *to the Orchestrator*, which decides what happens next. So single-responsibility + decoupling is intact — precisely the design you described.

## The clean layering
```
Orchestrator   ← the ONLY thing that knows both agents + owns the loop (the bridge)
   ├─ Developer agent   (job: build)      — knows nothing of the tester
   └─ Tester agent      (job: verify)     — knows nothing of the developer
        └─ both use shared Tools (write_file, run_in_sandbox)  — tools know nothing of agents
```
This is the standard rule: **agents call tools; the orchestrator coordinates agents; agents never call agents.** That's what's in place.

## One honest SRP nuance (since you care about Uncle Bob)
The coordination logic (`verifyAndFix`) currently lives *inside* `Orchestrator.java` — and the Orchestrator already does a few things (single-step dispatch, parallel-plan execution, and now the verify-fix loop). Strictly by single-responsibility, that loop could be its own class — say a `BuildVerifier` / `SelfCorrectingBuildCoordinator` — that the Orchestrator delegates to. The *behavior* is already correctly decoupled (agents don't know each other); this would just keep the Orchestrator itself lean and give the loop its own home.

Nothing's broken — it works and the agents are properly separated. But if you want the cleaner separation, I can extract that loop into its own coordinator class (pure refactor, no behavior change). Want me to, or leave it in the Orchestrator for now?"

"shay":"the loop is part of the orchastrator so it only makes sence it will be in the same class"

"jarvis":"Agreed — that's a sound call, and arguably the more correct reading of SRP.

The Orchestrator's single responsibility *is* **coordinating agents**. The verify-fix loop is coordination — deciding who runs, in what order, and reacting to results. So it belongs right there. Splitting it out would just scatter one cohesive responsibility across two classes for no real gain.

(The only time I'd revisit is if the Orchestrator grew so large that "coordination" started covering genuinely different concerns — but a build-verify loop isn't that; it's core orchestration.)

So: **nothing to change.** The loop stays in the Orchestrator, the agents stay decoupled, and you've got clean separation. 👍"

## What Jarvis is
A **local-first agentic AI OS** on your Mac: voice/text in → the Brain picks an agent → the agent uses real tools through a real model loop → acts on your machine → reports back. Everything runs locally; nothing leaves the box unless you opt into a cloud provider. **Built across 8 phases + dozens of refinements — ~100 work items done.**

## The architecture (all real, not scaffolding)
```
HUD (React) ── one cognitive door ── Brain/Orchestrator ── Agents ── Tools/Capabilities
                                          │                   │
                                     Model layer        Sandbox · Files · Memory · Connectors · MCP
```

## ✅ Fully built & working
**Brain & agents**
- Orchestrator (single-step + multi-agent DAG planner, parallel dependency-gated execution)
- Model-driven agent router (~35-agent roster, categorized) + per-task model selection
- Agent runtime (real tool-calling loop) + the self-correcting build loop

**Capabilities (the things it can DO)** — files (read/write/search + rich viewers), web search + fetch, calculate, KB semantic search, memory, About-Me profile, macOS actions (open app, reveal, clipboard, screenshot, spotlight), doc gen (PDF/Word/diagram/OCR), image convert, projects (build/run via sandbox), `install_app`, connectors, daily digest, backups.

**Intelligence/economics** — multi-provider adapters (Mock/Ollama/OpenAI/Anthropic) with per-call switching, token-budget governor + kill-switch, prompt caching, result cache, cheap-model routing.

**Safety** — risk classifier, permission modes, restrictions policy (hard blocks), sandbox runtime, Secrets Vault (AES), approval center, honesty guard, no-code-in-chat guarantee.

**Memory & continuity** — SQLite + Flyway, conversation continuity, agentic auto-memory, KB embeddings.

**Ops/observability** — audit log, trace IDs, agent-run traces, token/cost dashboard, debugger/replay, live system monitor (SSE), notification center + bell.

**Workflows** — durable multi-step engine, cron scheduler, triggers, webhook server, approval gates, retries.

**Interfaces** — the HUD (sphere, floating windows, ⌘K, ~18 windows), voice (STT/TTS/wake-word/PTT/barge-in/short-spoken), Telegram bridge.

**Extensibility** — Plugin SDK (hot-load JARs) + marketplace; MCP client (real JSON-RPC).

## 🟡 Partial / dormant (built, needs a flip to activate)
- **Telegram bridge** — wired, dormant until you paste a BotFather token
- **MCP servers** — client is real, but no servers configured (`servers: []`)
- **Docker sandbox isolation** — falls back to lightweight (no Docker installed)
- **OAuth connectors** — GitHub (PAT) + Slack/Discord (webhook) real; Gmail/Calendar/Drive/Notion scaffolded, need credentials

## ⛔ Not built (parked, queryable via the backlog memory)
Trace-driven self-learning router · ReAct/CodeAct strategy · more OAuth connectors · media/image generation + vision · EMAIL_RECEIVED trigger · per-plugin sandboxing · remote plugin registry · Whisper/ElevenLabs voice.

## Overall maturity
**The spine, brain, safety, memory, workflows, observability, capabilities, and HUD are all real and working** — roughly **~75% of the full vision**, and the architecture for the rest is in place. It's a genuinely functional local AI OS, not a demo.

## The single gating reality
Quality everywhere is **model-bound**. On the **14B test-period model** it's capable-but-modest. The framework is **provider-agnostic and Claude-ready** — the day you switch to Claude API, the *whole system* (not just builds — routing, reasoning, fixing, conversation, planning) levels up with **nothing left to wire**.

**Bottom line:** Jarvis is a real, end-to-end, agentic local AI OS — voice/text → think → act on your machine → remember → self-correct — with safety rails and a clean architecture. What's left is mostly **activation** (keys/tokens/Docker) and **model horsepower** (the Claude switch), not missing foundations."

## 🥇 The standout for *you*: a Privacy Router / PII guard
Your own rule (from your About-Me): *"sensitive personal/financial/work details should not be given to another AI model unless absolutely necessary."* That's a feature, not just a note. Jarvis could **detect sensitive content** (financial, credentials, personal) and **automatically keep it on the local model** — or redact it before any cloud call — and tell you when it did. Foundation already exists (ModelRouter `PRIVATE` mode, RestrictionPolicy, local Ollama). This is the most on-brand, distinctive thing I can think of for you: an AI OS that *enforces your privacy line by itself.*

## 🥈 Self-extending skills — "Jarvis, learn to do X"
This is the purest "agent not robot" idea, and you've *already built the pieces*: Jarvis can write code, run/test it in the sandbox, and hot-load plugins (Plugin SDK). So: *"Jarvis, learn to convert my invoices to CSV"* → it scaffolds a plugin, tests it via the build loop, installs it, and now it's a permanent capability. **Jarvis grows its own abilities** instead of you adding code. Shines on Claude.

## 🥉 Proactive watchers (the "heartbeat")
Turn Jarvis from reactive to proactive — the thing that separates an assistant from a chatbot:
- File watcher: new download/invoice → propose filing/renaming
- Threshold nudges: "disk 90% full", "3 failed builds today"
- Calendar/time triggers: a pushed morning brief (you have `daily_digest` + Telegram — just needs scheduling to fire it)

Agentic version: watchers *propose* an action to the Notification Center; you approve.

## More, by theme
- **Vision / screen-aware** *(needs a vision model)* — "look at my screen and fix this error", read a screenshot, UI QA. Multimodal jump.
- **Personal second-brain RAG** — continuously auto-index Documents/Notes → "what was I working on?", "find that doc about pricing." (KB + embeddings exist; this makes it *ambient*.)
- **Dev-workflow agent** *(fits your day job)* — PR review, run the test suite, triage GitHub issues — via the connector framework + `run_in_sandbox`.
- **Preference-learning loop** — Jarvis quietly updates your About-Me profile from interactions (with consent), so it gets more "you" over time.
- **"Undo that" / task replay** — conversational undo on top of the rollback recipes you already have.

## Already covered (so we don't repeat)
High-value but **already parked** in the backlog: MCP browser automation, trace-driven self-learning router, more OAuth connectors, image/media generation, Whisper/ElevenLabs voice, OpenAI-compatible endpoint. (Ask "what's parked?" anytime.)

"jarvis":"Both issues are fixed and verified. Here's what was wrong and what I did:

## Two separate problems — both fixed

### 1. "What's my name?" failing (the real bug)
Your profile (`about-me.md`) is complete — **Name: Shay Marko** is right there. But `ContextBuilder` was injecting **nothing about you** into each conversation. The profile was only reachable if the model *chose* to call the `profile_search` tool — and the local 14B model often skips that. I reproduced it: fresh sessions answered correctly only **~1 in 3 times**.

**Fix:** `ProfileService.compactIdentity()` now pulls just your **Identity + Snapshot** (name, location, languages — capped at 900 chars), and `ContextBuilder` injects that **every turn**. Your deeper/sensitive sections (financial, reserve-duty, etc.) stay out of the always-on block and remain on-demand via `profile_search` — so it's both reliable *and* privacy-preserving. I also firmed up the framing so the model treats it as authoritative.

→ Now **5/5 fresh sessions** answer *"Your name is Shay Marko."*

### 2. Memory window not showing your About-Me
These are **two different things** in Jarvis:
- **Profile** (`about-me.md`) = the single "who I am" doc.
- **Memory** = discrete facts/notes.

The Profile had **no UI** — you opened Memory and only saw discrete facts. **Fix:** the Memory window now has a collapsible **"Your profile · who Jarvis knows you are"** editor at the top (loads/saves `about-me.md`, with Save/Discard), above the "Trusted memory" list.

```
◈ request   "who am I"
├─ ◍ intent   Understood the request
├─ ◍ intent   Recalled 6 prior turns
├─ ⬡ agent    Routed to Knowledge Librarian
│   ├─ ◆ model   qwen2.5-coder:14b
│   └─ ▸ tool    profile_search · name
└─ ✓ answer   Composed the answer
```

The hierarchy is meaningful, not cosmetic:
- **Root** = your prompt
- **intent / answer** = top-level branches (pre-processing & final result)
- **each agent** is a branch, with its **model + tool calls nested underneath it** — and in a multi-agent Planner run, each agent gets its own branch with its own tools.

Each node has a colored glyph badge per kind (◈ root · ◍ intent · ⬡ agent · ◆ model · ▸ tool · ✓ answer · ✕ error). Your Steps/Tree choice is remembered across sessions.
